[2026-05-29T21:59:24.227Z] [INFO]       "content": "# formal-ai\n\nFormal AI is a Rust implementation of a symbolic, deterministic assistant that exposes OpenAI-shaped interfaces without neural-network inference.\n\nThe current implementation covers the surface area requested in issue #1:\n\n- library API for symbolic prompt handling\n- CLI chat command\n- HTTP API server with `/v1/chat/completions` and `/v1/responses`\n- Telegram bot CLI with long polling by default and an opt-in webhook server, configured through [`lino-arguments`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments)\n- human-readable Links Notation knowledge and dataset export through `lino-objects-codec`\n- Docker-in-Docker Telegram bot image based on `konard/box-dind:2.1.1`\n- GitHub Pages markdown chat demo backed by a Rust-generated WebAssembly worker\n- Electron desktop shell that starts the local Rust HTTP API and reuses the web chat\n\nProject direction is tracked in [VISION.md](VISION.md), [GOALS.md](GOALS.md), and [NON-GOALS.md](NON-GOALS.md). Implementation progress against the vision is tracked in [ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md). The issue #12 synthesis is in [docs/case-studies/issue-12/README.md](docs/case-studies/issue-12/README.md).\n\n## Universal Problem-Solving Algorithm\n\nEvery prompt — a greeting, a math question, a translation, a \"write a program\"\nrequest, an agent action, or something the assistant has never seen — walks the\nsame loop. The skeleton is **decompose the problem into tasks, derive a test\n(experiment) for each task, draft candidate solutions, select the smallest\nsufficient draft, and compose the drafts back into one solution**:\n\n![Universal Problem Solving Algorithm: a problem fans out into tasks, each task into tests, each test into candidate drafts, and the selected drafts compose back into a single solution](docs/assets/universal-problem-solving-algorithm.jpg)\n\nThis is not a separate subsystem bolted onto a catalogue of intents — it is the\nmain solver path. `src/solver.rs::UniversalSolver` runs the same 11-step loop\nfor every impulse:\n\n| Diagram stage | Loop steps in `src/solver.rs` |\n| --- | --- |\n| `Problem` | 1. Impulse, 2. Formalization (Links-Notation intent), 3. Context, 4. History lookup |\n| `decomposition → tasks` | 5. Decomposition into sub-impulses (`record_decomposition`) |\n| `experiment → tests` | 6. TDD-style validation (`record_validation`) |\n| `selection → drafts` | 7. Solution synthesis (`record_candidates`), 8. Combination of the smallest sufficient candidate |\n| `composition → solutions → Solution` | 9. Verification, 10. Simplification, 11. Documentation with a `trace:` pointer |\n\nEvery step appends its own event to the append-only log, so the chat can answer\n\"why did you do that?\" from the recorded experience. The full algorithm,\nincluding the reasoning-under-unknowns path (`src/solver_unknown_reasoning.rs`)\nand intent formalization (`src/intent_formalization.rs`), is documented in\n[VISION.md](VISION.md#universal-problem-solving-algorithm) and\n[ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md). How much of the vision is built versus still\nplanned — including the open industry-benchmark coverage gap — is tracked in\n[ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md).\n\n## Quick Start\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Hi\"\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Write me hello world program in Rust\" --format chat\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"What is 8% of $50?\"\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Посчитай 1000 рублей в долларах\"\ncargo run -- dataset\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\ncargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\nnpm install --prefix desktop\nnpm run desktop:dev\nTELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc cargo run -- telegram                       # long polling (default)\ncargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080      # webhook server (opt-in)\nrust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\nexperiments/verify-hello-world-examples.sh\n```\n\nExample API call:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/chat/completions \\\n  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n  -d '{\"model\":\"formal-symbolic-production\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"Hi\"}]}'\n```\n\nTo require bearer authentication on `/v1/*` routes, set\n`FORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN` before starting the server and send the matching\nheader:\n\n```bash\nFORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN=local-test-token cargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/models \\\n  -H 'authorization: Bearer local-test-token'\n```\n\nExample Telegram webhook update:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/telegram/webhook \\\n  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n  -d '{\"update_id\":1,\"message\":{\"message_id\":7,\"date\":1,\"chat\":{\"id\":42,\"type\":\"private\"},\"text\":\"Write me hello world program in Rust\"}}'\n```\n\nDocker-in-Docker Telegram bot image:\n\n```bash\ndocker build -t formal-ai .\ndocker run --rm --privileged \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  -v formal-ai-docker:/var/lib/docker \\\n  formal-ai\n\n# Preferred when Sysbox is available:\ndocker run --rm --runtime=sysbox-runc \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  -v formal-ai-docker:/var/lib/docker \\\n  formal-ai\n```\n\nThe root image is intentionally the only supported Docker runtime: it inherits\nfrom `konard/box-dind:2.1.1`, starts `/usr/local/bin/dind-entrypoint.sh`, and\ndefaults to `formal-ai telegram --mode polling`. Do not bind-mount the host\n`/var/run/docker.sock`; the image expects its own inner Docker daemon and uses\n`/var/lib/docker` for that daemon's storage.\n\nVerify the container contract and the `start-command` Docker isolation wrapper:\n\n```bash\ndocker run --rm --privileged formal-ai verify-formal-ai-dind\ndocker run --rm --privileged formal-ai bash -lc \\\n  '$ --isolated docker --auto-remove-docker-container -- echo formal-ai-dind-ok'\n```\n\nThe static demo lives in `src/web/index.html`. Serve it from a local web server or GitHub Pages so the WebAssembly worker can be fetched by the browser. The demo starts with a user greeting, renders markdown in messages, previews markdown input, and includes a randomized dialog mode for hello-world prompts across several programming languages. Rust/WASM owns parity-sensitive worker primitives such as prompt normalization, language detection, arithmetic evaluation, stable ids, intent-route matching, unknown-answer variation, and web-search fusion; JavaScript remains responsible for UI state, seed fetching, browser fetch/CORS orchestration, and no-WASM fallbacks. Browser JavaScript dependencies are prebundled with Bun into `src/web/vendor.bundle.js`; run `bun install` once and `bun run build:web` after changing web dependencies. The companion connectivity diagnostics page lives in `src/web/tests/index.html` and is deployed at `/formal-ai/tests/`; it checks direct browser fetches, public knowledge APIs, iframe embeddability, and a configurable local `web-capture` proxy.\n\n### Desktop app\n\nThe desktop app lives in [`desktop/`](desktop/) and follows the same boundary as the browser and HTTP server. Electron starts a loopback `formal-ai serve` process, serves `src/web/` from a local static server, and loads the existing chat UI with a preload bridge that reports the API, graph, memory, and permission status.\n\n```bash\nnpm install --prefix desktop\ncargo build\nnpm run desktop:dev\nnpm run desktop:smoke\n```\n\nIn desktop mode, prompt sends use `POST /v1/chat/completions` on the local Rust API, and the network link points to `GET /v1/graph`. The same **Export memory** and **Import memory** controls read and write the full `formal_ai_bundle`; no separate desktop memory format exists. Agent mode remains off by default, and the desktop sidebar shows whether agent/tool-call actions are permission-gated or explicitly opted in.\n\nPackaging starts from the same shell:\n\n```bash\ncargo build --release\nnpm --prefix desktop run build\n```\n\nSet `FORMAL_AI_DESKTOP_BINARY=/path/to/formal-ai` before packaging to bundle a specific binary. Release builds copy the web assets and seed mirror into `desktop/dist-web/`, copy the binary into `desktop/bin/` when available, and produce OS artifacts under `desktop/release/`.\n\n### Full-memory export and import\n\nEvery interface produces the same self-contained Links Notation document by default. In the browser, the **Export memory** topbar button writes `formal-ai-memory.lino` as a complete `formal_ai_bundle` — the entire seed (rules, concepts, tools, multilingual responses), UI preferences, environment metadata, and the full append-only event log — so a single click is enough to reconstitute the session. **Import memory** auto-detects bundle vs legacy `demo_memory` files and surfaces migration suggestions when the imported seed version differs from the running app's. The CLI matches:\n\nNative Rust builds now select `doublets-rs` by default through the\n`doublets-native` feature. Links Notation stays the recovery and migration\nprojection: existing `demo_memory` logs and full `formal_ai_bundle` exports\nimport into the native store, export back to deterministic `.lino`, and can\nstill be handled by compiling with `--no-default-features` when a pure\n`MemoryStore` projection is needed.\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path full.lino           # default: full bundle\ncargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path events.lino --events-only  # legacy demo_memory\ncargo run -- memory import --path full.lino --into memory.lino           # accepts either format\ncargo run -- memory purge-deleted --path memory.lino --backup before-purge.lino --confirm\ncargo run -- memory reset --path memory.lino --backup before-reset.lino --confirm\ncargo run -- bundle export --path bundle.lino --memory memory.lino\ncargo run -- bundle import --path bundle.lino --into memory.lino\n```\n\nMemory normally remains append-only: deleting a conversation first records a\n`conversation_deleted` event and hides the thread. The explicit\n`purge-deleted` maintenance action physically removes every event for those\nsoft-deleted conversations, and `reset` clears the event log completely. Both\nbrowser actions show an export-first prompt and then an irreversible\nconfirmation prompt. The CLI refuses both destructive commands unless\n`--confirm` is present, and `--backup` writes a full `formal_ai_bundle` before\nthe memory file is changed.\n\nThe Rust library re-exports the same helpers — `export_memory_full`, `import_memory_full`, `suggest_memory_migrations`, `BundleInfo`, `ParsedBundle` — so embedders writing their own surface get the same defaults. The prefilled **Report issue** link records the dialog as a single compact `U:`/`A:` code block and points to [`docs/upload-memory.md`](docs/upload-memory.md) for attaching the full memory export (GitHub Gist or `.zip` workflow, plus redaction reminders) instead of repeating those instructions inline.\n\n### Teaching behavior in chat\n\nThe chat surface can explain and modify behavior rules without leaving the dialog. Behavior is surfaced as a series of `When X then Y` (or `When X do Y`) statements grouped by topic, and the same grammar can also update the dialog:\n\n```text\nList behavior rules\nShow behavior rule unknown\nList all facts you know about yourself\nWhen `Какая у тебя модель личности?` then `У меня символьная модель личности.`\nWhen I say `Какая у тебя модель личности?`, answer `У меня символьная модель личности.`\n```\n\n`List behavior rules` shows the current built-in routing rules, grouped by topic (Greetings, Farewells, Identity, Capabilities, Hello-world programs, Unknown fallback) and rendered as `When X then Y` statements. `Show behavior rule unknown` renders one rule as Links Notation with its topic, intent, match condition, response, source, and the canonical `when_then` statement. The `When X then Y` and `When X do Y` forms (and the explicit `When I say ... answer ...` form) record an append-only, dialog-local override, so the same prompt can answer differently in that conversation. The grammar is recognized in English (`When ... then ...`, `When ... do ...`, `If I ask ... reply ...`), Russian (`Когда ... тогда ...`, `Когда ... делай ...`, `Если ... то ...`), Hindi (`जब ... तब ...`, `जब ... तो ...`), and Chinese (`当 ... 时 ...`, `当 ... 则 ...`). Use **Export memory** to preserve that rule message with the session, or **Report issue** when the fact or rule should become part of the built-in seed.\n\n## Telegram Bot\n\nThe `formal-ai telegram` subcommand defaults to long polling and keeps the webhook server available as an opt-in mode. The CLI is configured through [`lino-arguments`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments) (a clap-compatible derive), so every flag also reads from the matching environment variable and from `.lenv`/`.env` files in the working directory.\n\n### Long polling (default)\n\n```bash\nexport TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc\ncargo run -- telegram                                                   # polling by default\ncargo run -- telegram --mode polling \\\n  --timeout 30 --limit 100 \\\n  --allowed-updates message,edited_message\n```\n\nThe polling client shells out to `curl`, calls Telegram's `getUpdates`, advances the offset after each batch, and replies through `sendMessage` with HTML formatting. The same `FormalAiEngine` used by the library, HTTP API, and web demo is reused, so polling answers match the other surfaces.\n\nThe Docker image uses this polling command as its default `CMD`, so the\ncontainer starts the bot as soon as `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` is set. Commands that\nneed nested containers should run through the bundled `$` wrapper with\n`--isolated docker`; `start-command` records command output and metadata under\n`/tmp/start-command/logs/`. Issue #195 called this `--isolation docker`; the\ncurrent Link Foundation Start CLI documents the flag as `--isolated docker`.\n\n### Webhook (opt-in)\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n# or equivalently for backwards compatibility:\ncargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n\n# Docker override for webhook mode:\ndocker run --rm --privileged -p 8080:8080 \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  formal-ai formal-ai telegram --mode webhook --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080\n```\n\nExpose the server through HTTPS and register the endpoint with Telegram:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s \"https://api.telegram.org/bot${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}/setWebhook\" \\\n  -d \"url=https://example.com/telegram/webhook\"\n```\n\nThe webhook accepts Telegram `message`, `edited_message`, `channel_post`, and `edited_channel_post` updates. It returns a direct Telegram `sendMessage` response for both private chats and group/channel chat IDs, using Telegram HTML formatting so code blocks survive the chat surface. This implementation does not store a bot token or perform outbound Telegram API calls from the webhook path; large file attachments require an outbound bot-client layer.\n\n## Rust Library\n\n```rust\nuse formal_ai::{create_chat_completion, ChatCompletionRequest, ChatMessage, MessageContent};\n\nlet request = ChatCompletionRequest {\n    model: None,\n    messages: vec![ChatMessage {\n        role: String::from(\"user\"),\n        content: MessageContent::Text(String::from(\"Hi\")),\n    }],\n    stream: false,\n    temperature: None,\n};\n\nlet completion = create_chat_completion(&request);\nassert_eq!(\n    completion.choices[0].message.content.plain_text(),\n    \"Hi, how may I help you?\"\n);\n```\n\n## Current Symbolic Behavior\n\nThe engine normalizes a prompt, selects a deterministic symbolic rule, and returns the rule output with evidence link identifiers and indented Links Notation. It can also consume an explicit `ProbabilityStore`: append-only Bayesian-style evidence and Markov transition evidence rank symbolic candidate IDs before the temperature / clarify-vs-guess policy runs. This stays non-neural; evidence is Links Notation data with provenance, timestamps, cached-source fingerprints, and deterministic replay.\n\nSeed rules currently cover:\n\n- greetings and polite follow-ups: `Hi`, `Hello`, `Hey`, `I am fine, thank you`, `thanks`\n- hello world requests for Rust, Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, and C\n- open-ended software artifact requests such as extensions, plugins, bots, apps, and tools, first returning a Links Notation meaning record with a requirement graph, subtasks, delivery mode, approval gates, reasoning, and plan steps, then returning language-aware starter domain code after the user approves the plan\n- calculator-parsable math, unit, currency, percentage, and datetime prompts through `link-calculator`, with the local arithmetic evaluator retained for unsupported word-operator and binary-modulo syntax\n- URL requests such as `Navigate to github.com`, `fetch example.com`, and `Сделай запрос к google.com`; navigation prompts check CORS-readable frame-policy metadata and render an iframe only when `X-Frame-Options` and CSP `frame-ancestors` do not block embedding, while explicit fetch prompts attempt a browser `fetch()` first and use the same frame-policy check before any embedded fallback\n- web-search, information-search, and implicit research prompts such as `Search the web for Nikola Tesla`, `Найди яблоко в интернете`, `Найди информацию о Rust программировании`, `Rust programming के बारे में जानकारी खोजो`, `查找关于 Rust 编程的信息`, and `What is the most popular dataset for translation quality validation?`; the browser demo queries DuckDuckGo, Internet Archive, Wikipedia, Wikidata, and Wiktionary, then returns reciprocal-rank-fused links\n- merged definition prompts such as `Merge Wikipedia definitions of IIR`, which combine localized definition blocks for the same seed/Wikidata concept, deduplicate repeated facts, and cite every source language; use `--definition-fusion auto`, `FORMAL_AI_DEFINITION_FUSION=auto`, or the browser Settings control to make plain prompts like `What is IIR?` use the same fusion path\n- generic project lookups for GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket repository URLs plus default-on promotion for matching `link-assistant`, `link-foundation`, and `linksplatform` projects such as `What is Hive Mind?`, `Что такое Hive Mind?`, and `What is link-cli?`; promoted answers are generated from `data/seed/projects.lino` through the deterministic `formalize → summarize → deformalize` pipeline in `src/summarization/` (split into `mod.rs`, `markdown.rs`, and `dialog.rs`), which also drives README ingestion, multi-turn dialog summaries, and chat-title generation (see [ARCHITECTURE.md § 7.1](ARCHITECTURE.md#71-project-lookups-and-summarization))\n- behavior-rule inspection and dialog-local rule updates through `List behavior rules` (grouped by topic, each rendered as a `When X then Y` statement), `Show behavior rule unknown`, and the multilingual `When ... then ...` / `When ... do ...` / `When I say ... answer ...` grammar\n- unknown prompts, which return a larger learnable-rule fallback with exact commands for inspecting rules, teaching the current dialog, exporting memory, or reporting a missing built-in rule\n\nHello-world answers include execution metadata. Rust, Python, JavaScript, Go, and C examples are compiled or syntax-checked and run by the issue-8 local verification harness with captured output. TypeScript is returned with an explicit warning because `tsc` is not configured in the current repository runtime.\n\nNo GPU, neural network, remote model, or random sampling is used.\n\n## Dataset Seeds\n\nIssue #1 source indexes and seed prompts are stored as indented Links Notation in `data/`.\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\nrust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n```\n\nThe generator writes source, greeting, hello-world, and demo-dialog records. `.lino` files are kept below 1500 lines and validated by the unit tests.\n\n## Hive Mind Dataset Mining\n\nIssue #115 adds an operator script for mining GitHub evidence from\n`link-assistant/hive-mind` pull requests, issues, reviews, diffs, and Actions\nlogs into a case-study dataset. Keep this outside the seed tool registry; it is\na repository maintenance command, not an in-agent reasoning tool.\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --collect\n```\n\nThe script wraps `formal-ai github-logs plan|collect` with the focused Hive\nMind defaults used by `docs/case-studies/issue-115/`.\n\n## Development\n\n```bash\ncargo fmt --all -- --check\ncargo clippy --all-targets --all-features\ncargo test --all-features --verbose\ncargo test --doc --verbose\nrust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n```\n\nDecode an overlong prefilled GitHub issue URL into readable Markdown during\nreport-link triage:\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/decode-github-issue-url.rs --url 'https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/new?...'\n```\n\nSee [REQUIREMENTS.md](REQUIREMENTS.md) for the cumulative requirement matrix (now alongside [VISION.md](VISION.md)) and [docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md](docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md) for the collected research and implementation plan.\n",

[2026-05-29T21:59:25.156Z] [INFO]         "content": "25\t\n26\t![Universal Problem Solving Algorithm: a problem fans out into tasks, each task into tests, each test into candidate drafts, and the selected drafts compose back into a single solution](docs/assets/universal-problem-solving-algorithm.jpg)\n27\t\n28\tThis is not a separate subsystem bolted onto a catalogue of intents — it is the\n29\tmain solver path. `src/solver.rs::UniversalSolver` runs the same 11-step loop\n30\tfor every impulse:\n31\t\n32\t| Diagram stage | Loop steps in `src/solver.rs` |\n33\t| --- | --- |\n34\t| `Problem` | 1. Impulse, 2. Formalization (Links-Notation intent), 3. Context, 4. History lookup |\n35\t| `decomposition → tasks` | 5. Decomposition into sub-impulses (`record_decomposition`) |\n36\t| `experiment → tests` | 6. TDD-style validation (`record_validation`) |\n37\t| `selection → drafts` | 7. Solution synthesis (`record_candidates`), 8. Combination of the smallest sufficient candidate |\n38\t| `composition → solutions → Solution` | 9. Verification, 10. Simplification, 11. Documentation with a `trace:` pointer |\n39\t\n40\tEvery step appends its own event to the append-only log, so the chat can answer\n41\t\"why did you do that?\" from the recorded experience. The full algorithm,\n42\tincluding the reasoning-under-unknowns path (`src/solver_unknown_reasoning.rs`)\n43\tand intent formalization (`src/intent_formalization.rs`), is documented in\n44\t[VISION.md](VISION.md#universal-problem-solving-algorithm) and\n45\t[ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md). How much of the vision is built versus still\n46\tplanned — including the open industry-benchmark coverage gap — is tracked in\n47\t[ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md).\n48\t\n49\t## Quick Start\n50\t\n51\t```bash\n52\tcargo run -- chat --prompt \"Hi\"\n53\tcargo run -- chat --prompt \"Write me hello world program in Rust\" --format chat\n54\tcargo run -- chat --prompt \"What is 8% of $50?\"\n55\tcargo run -- chat --prompt \"Посчитай 1000 рублей в долларах\"\n56\tcargo run -- dataset\n57\trust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\n58\tcargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n59\tnpm install --prefix desktop\n60\tnpm run desktop:dev\n61\tTELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc cargo run -- telegram                       # long polling (default)\n62\tcargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080      # webhook server (opt-in)\n63\trust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\n64\texperiments/verify-hello-world-examples.sh\n65\t```\n66\t\n67\tExample API call:\n68\t\n69\t```bash\n70\tcurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/chat/completions \\\n71\t  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n72\t  -d '{\"model\":\"formal-symbolic-production\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"Hi\"}]}'\n73\t```\n74\t\n75\tTo require bearer authentication on `/v1/*` routes, set\n76\t`FORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN` before starting the server and send the matching\n77\theader:\n78\t\n79\t```bash\n80\tFORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN=local-test-token cargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n81\tcurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/models \\\n82\t  -H 'authorization: Bearer local-test-token'\n83\t```\n84\t\n85\tExample Telegram webhook update:\n86\t\n87\t```bash\n88\tcurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/telegram/webhook \\\n89\t  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n90\t  -d '{\"update_id\":1,\"message\":{\"message_id\":7,\"date\":1,\"chat\":{\"id\":42,\"type\":\"private\"},\"text\":\"Write me hello world program in Rust\"}}'\n91\t```\n92\t\n93\tDocker-in-Docker Telegram bot image:\n94\t\n95\t```bash\n96\tdocker build -t formal-ai .\n97\tdocker run --rm --privileged \\\n98\t  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n99\t  -v formal-ai-docker:/var/lib/docker \\\n100\t  formal-ai\n101\t\n102\t# Preferred when Sysbox is available:\n103\tdocker run --rm --runtime=sysbox-runc \\\n104\t  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n105\t  -v formal-ai-docker:/var/lib/docker \\\n106\t  formal-ai\n107\t```\n108\t\n109\tThe root image is intentionally the only supported Docker runtime: it inherits\n110\tfrom `konard/box-dind:2.1.1`, starts `/usr/local/bin/dind-entrypoint.sh`, and\n111\tdefaults to `formal-ai telegram --mode polling`. Do not bind-mount the host\n112\t`/var/run/docker.sock`; the image expects its own inner Docker daemon and uses\n113\t`/var/lib/docker` for that daemon's storage.\n114\t\n115\tVerify the container contract and the `start-command` Docker isolation wrapper:\n116\t\n117\t```bash\n118\tdocker run --rm --privileged formal-ai verify-formal-ai-dind\n119\tdocker run --rm --privileged formal-ai bash -lc \\\n120\t  '$ --isolated docker --auto-remove-docker-container -- echo formal-ai-dind-ok'\n121\t```\n122\t\n123\tThe static demo lives in `src/web/index.html`. Serve it from a local web server or GitHub Pages so the WebAssembly worker can be fetched by the browser. The demo starts with a user greeting, renders markdown in messages, previews markdown input, and includes a randomized dialog mode for hello-world prompts across several programming languages. Rust/WASM owns parity-sensitive worker primitives such as prompt normalization, language detection, arithmetic evaluation, stable ids, intent-route matching, unknown-answer variation, and web-search fusion; JavaScript remains responsible for UI state, seed fetching, browser fetch/CORS orchestration, and no-WASM fallbacks. Browser JavaScript dependencies are prebundled with Bun into `src/web/vendor.bundle.js`; run `bun install` once and `bun run build:web` after changing web dependencies. The companion connectivity diagnostics page lives in `src/web/tests/index.html` and is deployed at `/formal-ai/tests/`; it checks direct browser fetches, public knowledge APIs, iframe embeddability, and a configurable local `web-capture` proxy.\n124\t\n125\t### Desktop app\n126\t\n127\tThe desktop app lives in [`desktop/`](desktop/) and follows the same boundary as the browser and HTTP server. Electron starts a loopback `formal-ai serve` process, serves `src/web/` from a local static server, and loads the existing chat UI with a preload bridge that reports the API, graph, memory, and permission status.\n128\t\n129\t```bash\n130\tnpm install --prefix desktop\n131\tcargo build\n132\tnpm run desktop:dev\n133\tnpm run desktop:smoke\n134\t```\n135\t\n136\tIn desktop mode, prompt sends use `POST /v1/chat/completions` on the local Rust API, and the network link points to `GET /v1/graph`. The same **Export memory** and **Import memory** controls read and write the full `formal_ai_bundle`; no separate desktop memory format exists. Agent mode remains off by default, and the desktop sidebar shows whether agent/tool-call actions are permission-gated or explicitly opted in.\n137\t\n138\tPackaging starts from the same shell:\n139\t\n140\t```bash\n141\tcargo build --release\n142\tnpm --prefix desktop run build\n143\t```\n144\t\n145\tSet `FORMAL_AI_DESKTOP_BINARY=/path/to/formal-ai` before packaging to bundle a specific binary. Release builds copy the web assets and seed mirror into `desktop/dist-web/`, copy the binary into `desktop/bin/` when available, and produce OS artifacts under `desktop/release/`.\n146\t\n147\t### Full-memory export and import\n148\t\n149\tEvery interface produces the same self-contained Links Notation document by default. In the browser, the **Export memory** topbar button writes `formal-ai-memory.lino` as a complete `formal_ai_bundle` — the entire seed (rules, concepts, tools, multilingual responses), UI preferences, environment metadata, and the full append-only event log — so a single click is enough to reconstitute the session. **Import memory** auto-detects bundle vs legacy `demo_memory` files and surfaces migration suggestions when the imported seed version differs from the running app's. The CLI matches:\n150\t\n151\tNative Rust builds now select `doublets-rs` by default through the\n152\t`doublets-native` feature. Links Notation stays the recovery and migration\n153\tprojection: existing `demo_memory` logs and full `formal_ai_bundle` exports\n154\timport into the native store, export back to deterministic `.lino`, and can\n155\tstill be handled by compiling with `--no-default-features` when a pure\n156\t`MemoryStore` projection is needed.\n157\t\n158\t```bash\n159\tcargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path full.lino           # default: full bundle\n160\tcargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path events.lino --events-only  # legacy demo_memory\n161\tcargo run -- memory import --path full.lino --into memory.lino           # accepts either format\n162\tcargo run -- memory purge-deleted --path memory.lino --backup before-purge.lino --confirm\n163\tcargo run -- memory reset --path memory.lino --backup before-reset.lino --confirm\n164\tcargo run -- bundle export --path bundle.lino --memory memory.lino\n165\tcargo run -- bundle import --path bundle.lino --into memory.lino\n166\t```\n167\t\n168\tMemory normally remains append-only: deleting a conversation first records a\n169\t`conversation_deleted` event and hides the thread. The explicit\n170\t`purge-deleted` maintenance action physically removes every event for those\n171\tsoft-deleted conversations, and `reset` clears the event log completely. Both\n172\tbrowser actions show an export-first prompt and then an irreversible\n173\tconfirmation prompt. The CLI refuses both destructive commands unless\n174\t`--confirm` is present, and `--backup` writes a full `formal_ai_bundle` before\n175\tthe memory file is changed.\n176\t\n177\tThe Rust library re-exports the same helpers — `export_memory_full`, `import_memory_full`, `suggest_memory_migrations`, `BundleInfo`, `ParsedBundle` — so embedders writing their own surface get the same defaults. The prefilled **Report issue** link records the dialog as a single compact `U:`/`A:` code block and points to [`docs/upload-memory.md`](docs/upload-memory.md) for attaching the full memory export (GitHub Gist or `.zip` workflow, plus redaction reminders) instead of repeating those instructions inline.\n178\t\n179\t### Teaching behavior in chat\n180\t\n181\tThe chat surface can explain and modify behavior rules without leaving the dialog. Behavior is surfaced as a series of `When X then Y` (or `When X do Y`) statements grouped by topic, and the same grammar can also update the dialog:\n182\t\n183\t```text\n184\tList behavior rules\n185\tShow behavior rule unknown\n186\tList all facts you know about yourself\n187\tWhen `Какая у тебя модель личности?` then `У меня символьная модель личности.`\n188\tWhen I say `Какая у тебя модель личности?`, answer `У меня символьная модель личности.`\n189\t```\n190\t\n191\t`List behavior rules` shows the current built-in routing rules, grouped by topic (Greetings, Farewells, Identity, Capabilities, Hello-world programs, Unknown fallback) and rendered as `When X then Y` statements. `Show behavior rule unknown` renders one rule as Links Notation with its topic, intent, match condition, response, source, and the canonical `when_then` statement. The `When X then Y` and `When X do Y` forms (and the explicit `When I say ... answer ...` form) record an append-only, dialog-local override, so the same prompt can answer differently in that conversation. The grammar is recognized in English (`When ... then ...`, `When ... do ...`, `If I ask ... reply ...`), Russian (`Когда ... тогда ...`, `Когда ... делай ...`, `Если ... то ...`), Hindi (`जब ... तब ...`, `जब ... तो ...`), and Chinese (`当 ... 时 ...`, `当 ... 则 ...`). Use **Export memory** to preserve that rule message with the session, or **Report issue** when the fact or rule should become part of the built-in seed.\n192\t\n193\t## Telegram Bot\n194\t\n195\tThe `formal-ai telegram` subcommand defaults to long polling and keeps the webhook server available as an opt-in mode. The CLI is configured through [`lino-arguments`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments) (a clap-compatible derive), so every flag also reads from the matching environment variable and from `.lenv`/`.env` files in the working directory.\n196\t\n197\t### Long polling (default)\n198\t\n199\t```bash\n200\texport TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc\n201\tcargo run -- telegram                                                   # polling by default\n202\tcargo run -- telegram --mode polling \\\n203\t  --timeout 30 --limit 100 \\\n204\t  --allowed-updates message,edited_message\n205\t```\n206\t\n207\tThe polling client shells out to `curl`, calls Telegram's `getUpdates`, advances the offset after each batch, and replies through `sendMessage` with HTML formatting. The same `FormalAiEngine` used by the library, HTTP API, and web demo is reused, so polling answers match the other surfaces.\n208\t\n209\tThe Docker image uses this polling command as its default `CMD`, so the\n210\tcontainer starts the bot as soon as `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` is set. Commands that\n211\tneed nested containers should run through the bundled `$` wrapper with\n212\t`--isolated docker`; `start-command` records command output and metadata under\n213\t`/tmp/start-command/logs/`. Issue #195 called this `--isolation docker`; the\n214\tcurrent Link Foundation Start CLI documents the flag as `--isolated docker`.\n215\t\n216\t### Webhook (opt-in)\n217\t\n218\t```bash\n219\tcargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n220\t# or equivalently for backwards compatibility:\n221\tcargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n222\t\n223\t# Docker override for webhook mode:\n224\tdocker run --rm --privileged -p 8080:8080 \\\n225\t  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n226\t  formal-ai formal-ai telegram --mode webhook --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080\n227\t```\n228\t\n229\tExpose the server through HTTPS and register the endpoint with Telegram:\n230\t\n231\t```bash\n232\tcurl -s \"https://api.telegram.org/bot${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}/setWebhook\" \\\n233\t  -d \"url=https://example.com/telegram/webhook\"\n234\t```\n235\t\n236\tThe webhook accepts Telegram `message`, `edited_message`, `channel_post`, and `edited_channel_post` updates. It returns a direct Telegram `sendMessage` response for both private chats and group/channel chat IDs, using Telegram HTML formatting so code blocks survive the chat surface. This implementation does not store a bot token or perform outbound Telegram API calls from the webhook path; large file attachments require an outbound bot-client layer.\n237\t\n238\t## Rust Library\n239\t\n240\t```rust\n241\tuse formal_ai::{create_chat_completion, ChatCompletionRequest, ChatMessage, MessageContent};\n242\t\n243\tlet request = ChatCompletionRequest {\n244\t    model: None,\n245\t    messages: vec![ChatMessage {\n246\t        role: String::from(\"user\"),\n247\t        content: MessageContent::Text(String::from(\"Hi\")),\n248\t    }],\n249\t    stream: false,\n250\t    temperature: None,\n251\t};\n252\t\n253\tlet completion = create_chat_completion(&request);\n254\tassert_eq!(\n255\t    completion.choices[0].message.content.plain_text(),\n256\t    \"Hi, how may I help you?\"\n257\t);\n258\t```\n259\t\n260\t## Current Symbolic Behavior\n261\t\n262\tThe engine normalizes a prompt, selects a deterministic symbolic rule, and returns the rule output with evidence link identifiers and indented Links Notation. It can also consume an explicit `ProbabilityStore`: append-only Bayesian-style evidence and Markov transition evidence rank symbolic candidate IDs before the temperature / clarify-vs-guess policy runs. This stays non-neural; evidence is Links Notation data with provenance, timestamps, cached-source fingerprints, and deterministic replay.\n263\t\n264\tSeed rules currently cover:\n265\t\n266\t- greetings and polite follow-ups: `Hi`, `Hello`, `Hey`, `I am fine, thank you`, `thanks`\n267\t- hello world requests for Rust, Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, and C\n268\t- open-ended software artifact requests such as extensions, plugins, bots, apps, and tools, first returning a Links Notation meaning record with a requirement graph, subtasks, delivery mode, approval gates, reasoning, and plan steps, then returning language-aware starter domain code after the user approves the plan\n269\t- calculator-parsable math, unit, currency, percentage, and datetime prompts through `link-calculator`, with the local arithmetic evaluator retained for unsupported word-operator and binary-modulo syntax\n270\t- URL requests such as `Navigate to github.com`, `fetch example.com`, and `Сделай запрос к google.com`; navigation prompts check CORS-readable frame-policy metadata and render an iframe only when `X-Frame-Options` and CSP `frame-ancestors` do not block embedding, while explicit fetch prompts attempt a browser `fetch()` first and use the same frame-policy check before any embedded fallback\n271\t- web-search, information-search, and implicit research prompts such as `Search the web for Nikola Tesla`, `Найди яблоко в интернете`, `Найди информацию о Rust программировании`, `Rust programming के बारे में जानकारी खोजो`, `查找关于 Rust 编程的信息`, and `What is the most popular dataset for translation quality validation?`; the browser demo queries DuckDuckGo, Internet Archive, Wikipedia, Wikidata, and Wiktionary, then returns reciprocal-rank-fused links\n272\t- merged definition prompts such as `Merge Wikipedia definitions of IIR`, which combine localized definition blocks for the same seed/Wikidata concept, deduplicate repeated facts, and cite every source language; use `--definition-fusion auto`, `FORMAL_AI_DEFINITION_FUSION=auto`, or the browser Settings control to make plain prompts like `What is IIR?` use the same fusion path\n273\t- generic project lookups for GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket repository URLs plus default-on promotion for matching `link-assistant`, `link-foundation`, and `linksplatform` projects such as `What is Hive Mind?`, `Что такое Hive Mind?`, and `What is link-cli?`; promoted answers are generated from `data/seed/projects.lino` through the deterministic `formalize → summarize → deformalize` pipeline in `src/summarization/` (split into `mod.rs`, `markdown.rs`, and `dialog.rs`), which also drives README ingestion, multi-turn dialog summaries, and chat-title generation (see [ARCHITECTURE.md § 7.1](ARCHITECTURE.md#71-project-lookups-and-summarization))\n274\t- behavior-rule inspection and dialog-local rule updates through `List behavior rules` (grouped by topic, each rendered as a `When X then Y` statement), `Show behavior rule unknown`, and the multilingual `When ... then ...` / `When ... do ...` / `When I say ... answer ...` grammar\n275\t- unknown prompts, which return a larger learnable-rule fallback with exact commands for inspecting rules, teaching the current dialog, exporting memory, or reporting a missing built-in rule\n276\t\n277\tHello-world answers include execution metadata. Rust, Python, JavaScript, Go, and C examples are compiled or syntax-checked and run by the issue-8 local verification harness with captured output. TypeScript is returned with an explicit warning because `tsc` is not configured in the current repository runtime.\n278\t\n279\tNo GPU, neural network, remote model, or random sampling is used.\n280\t\n281\t## Dataset Seeds\n282\t\n283\tIssue #1 source indexes and seed prompts are stored as indented Links Notation in `data/`.\n284\t\n285\t```bash\n286\trust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\n287\trust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n288\t```\n289\t\n290\tThe generator writes source, greeting, hello-world, and demo-dialog records. `.lino` files are kept below 1500 lines and validated by the unit tests.\n291\t\n292\t## Hive Mind Dataset Mining\n293\t\n294\tIssue #115 adds an operator script for mining GitHub evidence from\n295\t`link-assistant/hive-mind` pull requests, issues, reviews, diffs, and Actions\n296\tlogs into a case-study dataset. Keep this outside the seed tool registry; it is\n297\ta repository maintenance command, not an in-agent reasoning tool.\n298\t\n299\t```bash\n300\trust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\n301\trust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --collect\n302\t```\n303\t\n304\tThe script wraps `formal-ai github-logs plan|collect` with the focused Hive\n305\tMind defaults used by `docs/case-studies/issue-115/`.\n306\t\n307\t## Development\n308\t\n309\t```bash\n310\tcargo fmt --all -- --check\n311\tcargo clippy --all-targets --all-features\n312\tcargo test --all-features --verbose\n313\tcargo test --doc --verbose\n314\trust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n315\t```\n316\t\n317\tDecode an overlong prefilled GitHub issue URL into readable Markdown during\n318\treport-link triage:\n319\t\n320\t```bash\n321\trust-script scripts/decode-github-issue-url.rs --url 'https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/new?...'\n322\t```\n323\t\n324\tSee [REQUIREMENTS.md](REQUIREMENTS.md) for the cumulative requirement matrix (now alongside [VISION.md](VISION.md)) and [docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md](docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md) for the collected research and implementation plan.\n325\t"

[2026-05-29T21:59:25.156Z] [INFO]       "content": "\n![Universal Problem Solving Algorithm: a problem fans out into tasks, each task into tests, each test into candidate drafts, and the selected drafts compose back into a single solution](docs/assets/universal-problem-solving-algorithm.jpg)\n\nThis is not a separate subsystem bolted onto a catalogue of intents — it is the\nmain solver path. `src/solver.rs::UniversalSolver` runs the same 11-step loop\nfor every impulse:\n\n| Diagram stage | Loop steps in `src/solver.rs` |\n| --- | --- |\n| `Problem` | 1. Impulse, 2. Formalization (Links-Notation intent), 3. Context, 4. History lookup |\n| `decomposition → tasks` | 5. Decomposition into sub-impulses (`record_decomposition`) |\n| `experiment → tests` | 6. TDD-style validation (`record_validation`) |\n| `selection → drafts` | 7. Solution synthesis (`record_candidates`), 8. Combination of the smallest sufficient candidate |\n| `composition → solutions → Solution` | 9. Verification, 10. Simplification, 11. Documentation with a `trace:` pointer |\n\nEvery step appends its own event to the append-only log, so the chat can answer\n\"why did you do that?\" from the recorded experience. The full algorithm,\nincluding the reasoning-under-unknowns path (`src/solver_unknown_reasoning.rs`)\nand intent formalization (`src/intent_formalization.rs`), is documented in\n[VISION.md](VISION.md#universal-problem-solving-algorithm) and\n[ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md). How much of the vision is built versus still\nplanned — including the open industry-benchmark coverage gap — is tracked in\n[ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md).\n\n## Quick Start\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Hi\"\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Write me hello world program in Rust\" --format chat\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"What is 8% of $50?\"\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Посчитай 1000 рублей в долларах\"\ncargo run -- dataset\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\ncargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\nnpm install --prefix desktop\nnpm run desktop:dev\nTELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc cargo run -- telegram                       # long polling (default)\ncargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080      # webhook server (opt-in)\nrust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\nexperiments/verify-hello-world-examples.sh\n```\n\nExample API call:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/chat/completions \\\n  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n  -d '{\"model\":\"formal-symbolic-production\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"Hi\"}]}'\n```\n\nTo require bearer authentication on `/v1/*` routes, set\n`FORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN` before starting the server and send the matching\nheader:\n\n```bash\nFORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN=local-test-token cargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/models \\\n  -H 'authorization: Bearer local-test-token'\n```\n\nExample Telegram webhook update:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/telegram/webhook \\\n  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n  -d '{\"update_id\":1,\"message\":{\"message_id\":7,\"date\":1,\"chat\":{\"id\":42,\"type\":\"private\"},\"text\":\"Write me hello world program in Rust\"}}'\n```\n\nDocker-in-Docker Telegram bot image:\n\n```bash\ndocker build -t formal-ai .\ndocker run --rm --privileged \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  -v formal-ai-docker:/var/lib/docker \\\n  formal-ai\n\n# Preferred when Sysbox is available:\ndocker run --rm --runtime=sysbox-runc \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  -v formal-ai-docker:/var/lib/docker \\\n  formal-ai\n```\n\nThe root image is intentionally the only supported Docker runtime: it inherits\nfrom `konard/box-dind:2.1.1`, starts `/usr/local/bin/dind-entrypoint.sh`, and\ndefaults to `formal-ai telegram --mode polling`. Do not bind-mount the host\n`/var/run/docker.sock`; the image expects its own inner Docker daemon and uses\n`/var/lib/docker` for that daemon's storage.\n\nVerify the container contract and the `start-command` Docker isolation wrapper:\n\n```bash\ndocker run --rm --privileged formal-ai verify-formal-ai-dind\ndocker run --rm --privileged formal-ai bash -lc \\\n  '$ --isolated docker --auto-remove-docker-container -- echo formal-ai-dind-ok'\n```\n\nThe static demo lives in `src/web/index.html`. Serve it from a local web server or GitHub Pages so the WebAssembly worker can be fetched by the browser. The demo starts with a user greeting, renders markdown in messages, previews markdown input, and includes a randomized dialog mode for hello-world prompts across several programming languages. Rust/WASM owns parity-sensitive worker primitives such as prompt normalization, language detection, arithmetic evaluation, stable ids, intent-route matching, unknown-answer variation, and web-search fusion; JavaScript remains responsible for UI state, seed fetching, browser fetch/CORS orchestration, and no-WASM fallbacks. Browser JavaScript dependencies are prebundled with Bun into `src/web/vendor.bundle.js`; run `bun install` once and `bun run build:web` after changing web dependencies. The companion connectivity diagnostics page lives in `src/web/tests/index.html` and is deployed at `/formal-ai/tests/`; it checks direct browser fetches, public knowledge APIs, iframe embeddability, and a configurable local `web-capture` proxy.\n\n### Desktop app\n\nThe desktop app lives in [`desktop/`](desktop/) and follows the same boundary as the browser and HTTP server. Electron starts a loopback `formal-ai serve` process, serves `src/web/` from a local static server, and loads the existing chat UI with a preload bridge that reports the API, graph, memory, and permission status.\n\n```bash\nnpm install --prefix desktop\ncargo build\nnpm run desktop:dev\nnpm run desktop:smoke\n```\n\nIn desktop mode, prompt sends use `POST /v1/chat/completions` on the local Rust API, and the network link points to `GET /v1/graph`. The same **Export memory** and **Import memory** controls read and write the full `formal_ai_bundle`; no separate desktop memory format exists. Agent mode remains off by default, and the desktop sidebar shows whether agent/tool-call actions are permission-gated or explicitly opted in.\n\nPackaging starts from the same shell:\n\n```bash\ncargo build --release\nnpm --prefix desktop run build\n```\n\nSet `FORMAL_AI_DESKTOP_BINARY=/path/to/formal-ai` before packaging to bundle a specific binary. Release builds copy the web assets and seed mirror into `desktop/dist-web/`, copy the binary into `desktop/bin/` when available, and produce OS artifacts under `desktop/release/`.\n\n### Full-memory export and import\n\nEvery interface produces the same self-contained Links Notation document by default. In the browser, the **Export memory** topbar button writes `formal-ai-memory.lino` as a complete `formal_ai_bundle` — the entire seed (rules, concepts, tools, multilingual responses), UI preferences, environment metadata, and the full append-only event log — so a single click is enough to reconstitute the session. **Import memory** auto-detects bundle vs legacy `demo_memory` files and surfaces migration suggestions when the imported seed version differs from the running app's. The CLI matches:\n\nNative Rust builds now select `doublets-rs` by default through the\n`doublets-native` feature. Links Notation stays the recovery and migration\nprojection: existing `demo_memory` logs and full `formal_ai_bundle` exports\nimport into the native store, export back to deterministic `.lino`, and can\nstill be handled by compiling with `--no-default-features` when a pure\n`MemoryStore` projection is needed.\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path full.lino           # default: full bundle\ncargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path events.lino --events-only  # legacy demo_memory\ncargo run -- memory import --path full.lino --into memory.lino           # accepts either format\ncargo run -- memory purge-deleted --path memory.lino --backup before-purge.lino --confirm\ncargo run -- memory reset --path memory.lino --backup before-reset.lino --confirm\ncargo run -- bundle export --path bundle.lino --memory memory.lino\ncargo run -- bundle import --path bundle.lino --into memory.lino\n```\n\nMemory normally remains append-only: deleting a conversation first records a\n`conversation_deleted` event and hides the thread. The explicit\n`purge-deleted` maintenance action physically removes every event for those\nsoft-deleted conversations, and `reset` clears the event log completely. Both\nbrowser actions show an export-first prompt and then an irreversible\nconfirmation prompt. The CLI refuses both destructive commands unless\n`--confirm` is present, and `--backup` writes a full `formal_ai_bundle` before\nthe memory file is changed.\n\nThe Rust library re-exports the same helpers — `export_memory_full`, `import_memory_full`, `suggest_memory_migrations`, `BundleInfo`, `ParsedBundle` — so embedders writing their own surface get the same defaults. The prefilled **Report issue** link records the dialog as a single compact `U:`/`A:` code block and points to [`docs/upload-memory.md`](docs/upload-memory.md) for attaching the full memory export (GitHub Gist or `.zip` workflow, plus redaction reminders) instead of repeating those instructions inline.\n\n### Teaching behavior in chat\n\nThe chat surface can explain and modify behavior rules without leaving the dialog. Behavior is surfaced as a series of `When X then Y` (or `When X do Y`) statements grouped by topic, and the same grammar can also update the dialog:\n\n```text\nList behavior rules\nShow behavior rule unknown\nList all facts you know about yourself\nWhen `Какая у тебя модель личности?` then `У меня символьная модель личности.`\nWhen I say `Какая у тебя модель личности?`, answer `У меня символьная модель личности.`\n```\n\n`List behavior rules` shows the current built-in routing rules, grouped by topic (Greetings, Farewells, Identity, Capabilities, Hello-world programs, Unknown fallback) and rendered as `When X then Y` statements. `Show behavior rule unknown` renders one rule as Links Notation with its topic, intent, match condition, response, source, and the canonical `when_then` statement. The `When X then Y` and `When X do Y` forms (and the explicit `When I say ... answer ...` form) record an append-only, dialog-local override, so the same prompt can answer differently in that conversation. The grammar is recognized in English (`When ... then ...`, `When ... do ...`, `If I ask ... reply ...`), Russian (`Когда ... тогда ...`, `Когда ... делай ...`, `Если ... то ...`), Hindi (`जब ... तब ...`, `जब ... तो ...`), and Chinese (`当 ... 时 ...`, `当 ... 则 ...`). Use **Export memory** to preserve that rule message with the session, or **Report issue** when the fact or rule should become part of the built-in seed.\n\n## Telegram Bot\n\nThe `formal-ai telegram` subcommand defaults to long polling and keeps the webhook server available as an opt-in mode. The CLI is configured through [`lino-arguments`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments) (a clap-compatible derive), so every flag also reads from the matching environment variable and from `.lenv`/`.env` files in the working directory.\n\n### Long polling (default)\n\n```bash\nexport TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc\ncargo run -- telegram                                                   # polling by default\ncargo run -- telegram --mode polling \\\n  --timeout 30 --limit 100 \\\n  --allowed-updates message,edited_message\n```\n\nThe polling client shells out to `curl`, calls Telegram's `getUpdates`, advances the offset after each batch, and replies through `sendMessage` with HTML formatting. The same `FormalAiEngine` used by the library, HTTP API, and web demo is reused, so polling answers match the other surfaces.\n\nThe Docker image uses this polling command as its default `CMD`, so the\ncontainer starts the bot as soon as `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` is set. Commands that\nneed nested containers should run through the bundled `$` wrapper with\n`--isolated docker`; `start-command` records command output and metadata under\n`/tmp/start-command/logs/`. Issue #195 called this `--isolation docker`; the\ncurrent Link Foundation Start CLI documents the flag as `--isolated docker`.\n\n### Webhook (opt-in)\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n# or equivalently for backwards compatibility:\ncargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n\n# Docker override for webhook mode:\ndocker run --rm --privileged -p 8080:8080 \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  formal-ai formal-ai telegram --mode webhook --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080\n```\n\nExpose the server through HTTPS and register the endpoint with Telegram:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s \"https://api.telegram.org/bot${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}/setWebhook\" \\\n  -d \"url=https://example.com/telegram/webhook\"\n```\n\nThe webhook accepts Telegram `message`, `edited_message`, `channel_post`, and `edited_channel_post` updates. It returns a direct Telegram `sendMessage` response for both private chats and group/channel chat IDs, using Telegram HTML formatting so code blocks survive the chat surface. This implementation does not store a bot token or perform outbound Telegram API calls from the webhook path; large file attachments require an outbound bot-client layer.\n\n## Rust Library\n\n```rust\nuse formal_ai::{create_chat_completion, ChatCompletionRequest, ChatMessage, MessageContent};\n\nlet request = ChatCompletionRequest {\n    model: None,\n    messages: vec![ChatMessage {\n        role: String::from(\"user\"),\n        content: MessageContent::Text(String::from(\"Hi\")),\n    }],\n    stream: false,\n    temperature: None,\n};\n\nlet completion = create_chat_completion(&request);\nassert_eq!(\n    completion.choices[0].message.content.plain_text(),\n    \"Hi, how may I help you?\"\n);\n```\n\n## Current Symbolic Behavior\n\nThe engine normalizes a prompt, selects a deterministic symbolic rule, and returns the rule output with evidence link identifiers and indented Links Notation. It can also consume an explicit `ProbabilityStore`: append-only Bayesian-style evidence and Markov transition evidence rank symbolic candidate IDs before the temperature / clarify-vs-guess policy runs. This stays non-neural; evidence is Links Notation data with provenance, timestamps, cached-source fingerprints, and deterministic replay.\n\nSeed rules currently cover:\n\n- greetings and polite follow-ups: `Hi`, `Hello`, `Hey`, `I am fine, thank you`, `thanks`\n- hello world requests for Rust, Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, and C\n- open-ended software artifact requests such as extensions, plugins, bots, apps, and tools, first returning a Links Notation meaning record with a requirement graph, subtasks, delivery mode, approval gates, reasoning, and plan steps, then returning language-aware starter domain code after the user approves the plan\n- calculator-parsable math, unit, currency, percentage, and datetime prompts through `link-calculator`, with the local arithmetic evaluator retained for unsupported word-operator and binary-modulo syntax\n- URL requests such as `Navigate to github.com`, `fetch example.com`, and `Сделай запрос к google.com`; navigation prompts check CORS-readable frame-policy metadata and render an iframe only when `X-Frame-Options` and CSP `frame-ancestors` do not block embedding, while explicit fetch prompts attempt a browser `fetch()` first and use the same frame-policy check before any embedded fallback\n- web-search, information-search, and implicit research prompts such as `Search the web for Nikola Tesla`, `Найди яблоко в интернете`, `Найди информацию о Rust программировании`, `Rust programming के बारे में जानकारी खोजो`, `查找关于 Rust 编程的信息`, and `What is the most popular dataset for translation quality validation?`; the browser demo queries DuckDuckGo, Internet Archive, Wikipedia, Wikidata, and Wiktionary, then returns reciprocal-rank-fused links\n- merged definition prompts such as `Merge Wikipedia definitions of IIR`, which combine localized definition blocks for the same seed/Wikidata concept, deduplicate repeated facts, and cite every source language; use `--definition-fusion auto`, `FORMAL_AI_DEFINITION_FUSION=auto`, or the browser Settings control to make plain prompts like `What is IIR?` use the same fusion path\n- generic project lookups for GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket repository URLs plus default-on promotion for matching `link-assistant`, `link-foundation`, and `linksplatform` projects such as `What is Hive Mind?`, `Что такое Hive Mind?`, and `What is link-cli?`; promoted answers are generated from `data/seed/projects.lino` through the deterministic `formalize → summarize → deformalize` pipeline in `src/summarization/` (split into `mod.rs`, `markdown.rs`, and `dialog.rs`), which also drives README ingestion, multi-turn dialog summaries, and chat-title generation (see [ARCHITECTURE.md § 7.1](ARCHITECTURE.md#71-project-lookups-and-summarization))\n- behavior-rule inspection and dialog-local rule updates through `List behavior rules` (grouped by topic, each rendered as a `When X then Y` statement), `Show behavior rule unknown`, and the multilingual `When ... then ...` / `When ... do ...` / `When I say ... answer ...` grammar\n- unknown prompts, which return a larger learnable-rule fallback with exact commands for inspecting rules, teaching the current dialog, exporting memory, or reporting a missing built-in rule\n\nHello-world answers include execution metadata. Rust, Python, JavaScript, Go, and C examples are compiled or syntax-checked and run by the issue-8 local verification harness with captured output. TypeScript is returned with an explicit warning because `tsc` is not configured in the current repository runtime.\n\nNo GPU, neural network, remote model, or random sampling is used.\n\n## Dataset Seeds\n\nIssue #1 source indexes and seed prompts are stored as indented Links Notation in `data/`.\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\nrust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n```\n\nThe generator writes source, greeting, hello-world, and demo-dialog records. `.lino` files are kept below 1500 lines and validated by the unit tests.\n\n## Hive Mind Dataset Mining\n\nIssue #115 adds an operator script for mining GitHub evidence from\n`link-assistant/hive-mind` pull requests, issues, reviews, diffs, and Actions\nlogs into a case-study dataset. Keep this outside the seed tool registry; it is\na repository maintenance command, not an in-agent reasoning tool.\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --collect\n```\n\nThe script wraps `formal-ai github-logs plan|collect` with the focused Hive\nMind defaults used by `docs/case-studies/issue-115/`.\n\n## Development\n\n```bash\ncargo fmt --all -- --check\ncargo clippy --all-targets --all-features\ncargo test --all-features --verbose\ncargo test --doc --verbose\nrust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n```\n\nDecode an overlong prefilled GitHub issue URL into readable Markdown during\nreport-link triage:\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/decode-github-issue-url.rs --url 'https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/new?...'\n```\n\nSee [REQUIREMENTS.md](REQUIREMENTS.md) for the cumulative requirement matrix (now alongside [VISION.md](VISION.md)) and [docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md](docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md) for the collected research and implementation plan.\n",

[2026-05-29T21:59:32.696Z] [INFO]         "content": "25\t\n26\t![Universal Problem Solving Algorithm: a problem fans out into tasks, each task into tests, each test into candidate drafts, and the selected drafts compose back into a single solution](docs/assets/universal-problem-solving-algorithm.jpg)\n27\t\n28\tThis is not a separate subsystem bolted onto a catalogue of intents — it is the\n29\tmain solver path. `src/solver.rs::UniversalSolver` runs the same 11-step loop\n30\tfor every impulse:\n31\t\n32\t| Diagram stage | Loop steps in `src/solver.rs` |\n33\t| --- | --- |\n34\t| `Problem` | 1. Impulse, 2. Formalization (Links-Notation intent), 3. Context, 4. History lookup |\n35\t| `decomposition → tasks` | 5. Decomposition into sub-impulses (`record_decomposition`) |\n36\t| `experiment → tests` | 6. TDD-style validation (`record_validation`) |\n37\t| `selection → drafts` | 7. Solution synthesis (`record_candidates`), 8. Combination of the smallest sufficient candidate |\n38\t| `composition → solutions → Solution` | 9. Verification, 10. Simplification, 11. Documentation with a `trace:` pointer |\n39\t\n40\tEvery step appends its own event to the append-only log, so the chat can answer\n41\t\"why did you do that?\" from the recorded experience. The full algorithm,\n42\tincluding the reasoning-under-unknowns path (`src/solver_unknown_reasoning.rs`)\n43\tand intent formalization (`src/intent_formalization.rs`), is documented in\n44\t[VISION.md](VISION.md#universal-problem-solving-algorithm) and\n45\t[ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md). [2026-05-29T21:59:32.696Z] [INFO]       "content": "\n![Universal Problem Solving Algorithm: a problem fans out into tasks, each task into tests, each test into candidate drafts, and the selected drafts compose back into a single solution](docs/assets/universal-problem-solving-algorithm.jpg)\n\nThis is not a separate subsystem bolted onto a catalogue of intents — it is the\nmain solver path. `src/solver.rs::UniversalSolver` runs the same 11-step loop\nfor every impulse:\n\n| Diagram stage | Loop steps in `src/solver.rs` |\n| --- | --- |\n| `Problem` | 1. Impulse, 2. Formalization (Links-Notation intent), 3. Context, 4. History lookup |\n| `decomposition → tasks` | 5. Decomposition into sub-impulses (`record_decomposition`) |\n| `experiment → tests` | 6. TDD-style validation (`record_validation`) |\n| `selection → drafts` | 7. Solution synthesis (`record_candidates`), 8. Combination of the smallest sufficient candidate |\n| `composition → solutions → Solution` | 9. Verification, 10. Simplification, 11. Documentation with a `trace:` pointer |\n\nEvery step appends its own event to the append-only log, so the chat can answer\n\"why did you do that?\" from the recorded experience. The full algorithm,\nincluding the reasoning-under-unknowns path (`src/solver_unknown_reasoning.rs`)\nand intent formalization (`src/intent_formalization.rs`), is documented in\n[VISION.md](VISION.md#universal-problem-solving-algorithm) and\n[ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md). How much of the vision is built versus still\nplanned — including the open industry-benchmark coverage gap — is tracked in\n[ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md).\n\n## Quick Start\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Hi\"\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Write me hello world program in Rust\" --format chat\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"What is 8% of $50?\"\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Посчитай 1000 рублей в долларах\"\ncargo run -- dataset\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\ncargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\nnpm install --prefix desktop\nnpm run desktop:dev\nTELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc cargo run -- telegram                       # long polling (default)\ncargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080      # webhook server (opt-in)\nrust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\nexperiments/verify-hello-world-examples.sh\n```\n\nExample API call:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/chat/completions \\\n  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n  -d '{\"model\":\"formal-symbolic-production\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"Hi\"}]}'\n```\n\nTo require bearer authentication on `/v1/*` routes, set\n`FORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN` before starting the server and send the matching\nheader:\n\n```bash\nFORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN=local-test-token cargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/models \\\n  -H 'authorization: Bearer local-test-token'\n```\n\nExample Telegram webhook update:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/telegram/webhook \\\n  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n  -d '{\"update_id\":1,\"message\":{\"message_id\":7,\"date\":1,\"chat\":{\"id\":42,\"type\":\"private\"},\"text\":\"Write me hello world program in Rust\"}}'\n```\n\nDocker-in-Docker Telegram bot image:\n\n```bash\ndocker build -t formal-ai .\ndocker run --rm --privileged \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  -v formal-ai-docker:/var/lib/docker \\",
