[2026-05-29T21:59:36.995Z] [INFO]         "type": "tool_result",
[2026-05-29T21:59:36.995Z] [INFO]         "content": "![Universal Problem Solving Algorithm: a problem fans out into tasks, each task into tests, each test into candidate drafts, and the selected drafts compose back into a single solution](docs/assets/universal-problem-solving-algorithm.jpg)\n\nThis is not a separate subsystem bolted onto a catalogue of intents — it is the\nmain solver path. `src/solver.rs::UniversalSolver` runs the same 11-step loop\nfor every impulse:\n\n| Diagram stage | Loop steps in `src/solver.rs` |\n| --- | --- |\n| `Problem` | 1. Impulse, 2. Formalization (Links-Notation intent), 3. Context, 4. History lookup |\n| `decomposition → tasks` | 5. Decomposition into sub-impulses (`record_decomposition`) |\n| `experiment → tests` | 6. TDD-style validation (`record_validation`) |\n| `selection → drafts` | 7. Solution synthesis (`record_candidates`), 8. Combination of the smallest sufficient candidate |\n| `composition → solutions → Solution` | 9. Verification, 10. Simplification, 11. Documentation with a `trace:` pointer |\n\nEvery step appends its own event to the append-only log, so the chat can answer\n\"why did you do that?\" from the recorded experience. The full algorithm,\nincluding the reasoning-under-unknowns path (`src/solver_unknown_reasoning.rs`)\nand intent formalization (`src/intent_formalization.rs`), is documented in\n[VISION.md](VISION.md#universal-problem-solving-algorithm) and\n[ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md). How much of the vision is built versus still\nplanned — including the open industry-benchmark coverage gap — is tracked in\n[ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md).\n\n## Quick Start\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Hi\"\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Write me hello world program in Rust\" --format chat\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"What is 8% of $50?\"\ncargo run -- chat --prompt \"Посчитай 1000 рублей в долларах\"\ncargo run -- dataset\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\ncargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\nnpm install --prefix desktop\nnpm run desktop:dev\nTELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc cargo run -- telegram                       # long polling (default)\ncargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080      # webhook server (opt-in)\nrust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\nexperiments/verify-hello-world-examples.sh\n```\n\nExample API call:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/chat/completions \\\n  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n  -d '{\"model\":\"formal-symbolic-production\",\"messages\":[{\"role\":\"user\",\"content\":\"Hi\"}]}'\n```\n\nTo require bearer authentication on `/v1/*` routes, set\n`FORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN` before starting the server and send the matching\nheader:\n\n```bash\nFORMAL_AI_API_BEARER_TOKEN=local-test-token cargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/v1/models \\\n  -H 'authorization: Bearer local-test-token'\n```\n\nExample Telegram webhook update:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s http://127.0.0.1:8080/telegram/webhook \\\n  -H 'content-type: application/json' \\\n  -d '{\"update_id\":1,\"message\":{\"message_id\":7,\"date\":1,\"chat\":{\"id\":42,\"type\":\"private\"},\"text\":\"Write me hello world program in Rust\"}}'\n```\n\nDocker-in-Docker Telegram bot image:\n\n```bash\ndocker build -t formal-ai .\ndocker run --rm --privileged \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  -v formal-ai-docker:/var/lib/docker \\\n  formal-ai\n\n# Preferred when Sysbox is available:\ndocker run --rm --runtime=sysbox-runc \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  -v formal-ai-docker:/var/lib/docker \\\n  formal-ai\n```\n\nThe root image is intentionally the only supported Docker runtime: it inherits\nfrom `konard/box-dind:2.1.1`, starts `/usr/local/bin/dind-entrypoint.sh`, and\ndefaults to `formal-ai telegram --mode polling`. Do not bind-mount the host\n`/var/run/docker.sock`; the image expects its own inner Docker daemon and uses\n`/var/lib/docker` for that daemon's storage.\n\nVerify the container contract and the `start-command` Docker isolation wrapper:\n\n```bash\ndocker run --rm --privileged formal-ai verify-formal-ai-dind\ndocker run --rm --privileged formal-ai bash -lc \\\n  '$ --isolated docker --auto-remove-docker-container -- echo formal-ai-dind-ok'\n```\n\nThe static demo lives in `src/web/index.html`. Serve it from a local web server or GitHub Pages so the WebAssembly worker can be fetched by the browser. The demo starts with a user greeting, renders markdown in messages, previews markdown input, and includes a randomized dialog mode for hello-world prompts across several programming languages. Rust/WASM owns parity-sensitive worker primitives such as prompt normalization, language detection, arithmetic evaluation, stable ids, intent-route matching, unknown-answer variation, and web-search fusion; JavaScript remains responsible for UI state, seed fetching, browser fetch/CORS orchestration, and no-WASM fallbacks. Browser JavaScript dependencies are prebundled with Bun into `src/web/vendor.bundle.js`; run `bun install` once and `bun run build:web` after changing web dependencies. The companion connectivity diagnostics page lives in `src/web/tests/index.html` and is deployed at `/formal-ai/tests/`; it checks direct browser fetches, public knowledge APIs, iframe embeddability, and a configurable local `web-capture` proxy.\n\n### Desktop app\n\nThe desktop app lives in [`desktop/`](desktop/) and follows the same boundary as the browser and HTTP server. Electron starts a loopback `formal-ai serve` process, serves `src/web/` from a local static server, and loads the existing chat UI with a preload bridge that reports the API, graph, memory, and permission status.\n\n```bash\nnpm install --prefix desktop\ncargo build\nnpm run desktop:dev\nnpm run desktop:smoke\n```\n\nIn desktop mode, prompt sends use `POST /v1/chat/completions` on the local Rust API, and the network link points to `GET /v1/graph`. The same **Export memory** and **Import memory** controls read and write the full `formal_ai_bundle`; no separate desktop memory format exists. Agent mode remains off by default, and the desktop sidebar shows whether agent/tool-call actions are permission-gated or explicitly opted in.\n\nPackaging starts from the same shell:\n\n```bash\ncargo build --release\nnpm --prefix desktop run build\n```\n\nSet `FORMAL_AI_DESKTOP_BINARY=/path/to/formal-ai` before packaging to bundle a specific binary. Release builds copy the web assets and seed mirror into `desktop/dist-web/`, copy the binary into `desktop/bin/` when available, and produce OS artifacts under `desktop/release/`.\n\n### Full-memory export and import\n\nEvery interface produces the same self-contained Links Notation document by default. In the browser, the **Export memory** topbar button writes `formal-ai-memory.lino` as a complete `formal_ai_bundle` — the entire seed (rules, concepts, tools, multilingual responses), UI preferences, environment metadata, and the full append-only event log — so a single click is enough to reconstitute the session. **Import memory** auto-detects bundle vs legacy `demo_memory` files and surfaces migration suggestions when the imported seed version differs from the running app's. The CLI matches:\n\nNative Rust builds now select `doublets-rs` by default through the\n`doublets-native` feature. Links Notation stays the recovery and migration\nprojection: existing `demo_memory` logs and full `formal_ai_bundle` exports\nimport into the native store, export back to deterministic `.lino`, and can\nstill be handled by compiling with `--no-default-features` when a pure\n`MemoryStore` projection is needed.\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path full.lino           # default: full bundle\ncargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path events.lino --events-only  # legacy demo_memory\ncargo run -- memory import --path full.lino --into memory.lino           # accepts either format\ncargo run -- memory purge-deleted --path memory.lino --backup before-purge.lino --confirm\ncargo run -- memory reset --path memory.lino --backup before-reset.lino --confirm\ncargo run -- bundle export --path bundle.lino --memory memory.lino\ncargo run -- bundle import --path bundle.lino --into memory.lino\n```\n\nMemory normally remains append-only: deleting a conversation first records a\n`conversation_deleted` event and hides the thread. The explicit\n`purge-deleted` maintenance action physically removes every event for those\nsoft-deleted conversations, and `reset` clears the event log completely. Both\nbrowser actions show an export-first prompt and then an irreversible\nconfirmation prompt. The CLI refuses both destructive commands unless\n`--confirm` is present, and `--backup` writes a full `formal_ai_bundle` before\nthe memory file is changed.\n\nThe Rust library re-exports the same helpers — `export_memory_full`, `import_memory_full`, `suggest_memory_migrations`, `BundleInfo`, `ParsedBundle` — so embedders writing their own surface get the same defaults. The prefilled **Report issue** link records the dialog as a single compact `U:`/`A:` code block and points to [`docs/upload-memory.md`](docs/upload-memory.md) for attaching the full memory export (GitHub Gist or `.zip` workflow, plus redaction reminders) instead of repeating those instructions inline.\n\n### Teaching behavior in chat\n\nThe chat surface can explain and modify behavior rules without leaving the dialog. Behavior is surfaced as a series of `When X then Y` (or `When X do Y`) statements grouped by topic, and the same grammar can also update the dialog:\n\n```text\nList behavior rules\nShow behavior rule unknown\nList all facts you know about yourself\nWhen `Какая у тебя модель личности?` then `У меня символьная модель личности.`\nWhen I say `Какая у тебя модель личности?`, answer `У меня символьная модель личности.`\n```\n\n`List behavior rules` shows the current built-in routing rules, grouped by topic (Greetings, Farewells, Identity, Capabilities, Hello-world programs, Unknown fallback) and rendered as `When X then Y` statements. `Show behavior rule unknown` renders one rule as Links Notation with its topic, intent, match condition, response, source, and the canonical `when_then` statement. The `When X then Y` and `When X do Y` forms (and the explicit `When I say ... answer ...` form) record an append-only, dialog-local override, so the same prompt can answer differently in that conversation. The grammar is recognized in English (`When ... then ...`, `When ... do ...`, `If I ask ... reply ...`), Russian (`Когда ... тогда ...`, `Когда ... делай ...`, `Если ... то ...`), Hindi (`जब ... तब ...`, `जब ... तो ...`), and Chinese (`当 ... 时 ...`, `当 ... 则 ...`). Use **Export memory** to preserve that rule message with the session, or **Report issue** when the fact or rule should become part of the built-in seed.\n\n## Telegram Bot\n\nThe `formal-ai telegram` subcommand defaults to long polling and keeps the webhook server available as an opt-in mode. The CLI is configured through [`lino-arguments`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments) (a clap-compatible derive), so every flag also reads from the matching environment variable and from `.lenv`/`.env` files in the working directory.\n\n### Long polling (default)\n\n```bash\nexport TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc\ncargo run -- telegram                                                   # polling by default\ncargo run -- telegram --mode polling \\\n  --timeout 30 --limit 100 \\\n  --allowed-updates message,edited_message\n```\n\nThe polling client shells out to `curl`, calls Telegram's `getUpdates`, advances the offset after each batch, and replies through `sendMessage` with HTML formatting. The same `FormalAiEngine` used by the library, HTTP API, and web demo is reused, so polling answers match the other surfaces.\n\nThe Docker image uses this polling command as its default `CMD`, so the\ncontainer starts the bot as soon as `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` is set. Commands that\nneed nested containers should run through the bundled `$` wrapper with\n`--isolated docker`; `start-command` records command output and metadata under\n`/tmp/start-command/logs/`. Issue #195 called this `--isolation docker`; the\ncurrent Link Foundation Start CLI documents the flag as `--isolated docker`.\n\n### Webhook (opt-in)\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n# or equivalently for backwards compatibility:\ncargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n\n# Docker override for webhook mode:\ndocker run --rm --privileged -p 8080:8080 \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  formal-ai formal-ai telegram --mode webhook --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080\n```\n\nExpose the server through HTTPS and register the endpoint with Telegram:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s \"https://api.telegram.org/bot${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}/setWebhook\" \\\n  -d \"url=https://example.com/telegram/webhook\"\n```\n\nThe webhook accepts Telegram `message`, `edited_message`, `channel_post`, and `edited_channel_post` updates. It returns a direct Telegram `sendMessage` response for both private chats and group/channel chat IDs, using Telegram HTML formatting so code blocks survive the chat surface. This implementation does not store a bot token or perform outbound Telegram API calls from the webhook path; large file attachments require an outbound bot-client layer.\n\n## Rust Library\n\n```rust\nuse formal_ai::{create_chat_completion, ChatCompletionRequest, ChatMessage, MessageContent};\n\nlet request = ChatCompletionRequest {\n    model: None,\n    messages: vec![ChatMessage {\n        role: String::from(\"user\"),\n        content: MessageContent::Text(String::from(\"Hi\")),\n    }],\n    stream: false,\n    temperature: None,\n};\n\nlet completion = create_chat_completion(&request);\nassert_eq!(\n    completion.choices[0].message.content.plain_text(),\n    \"Hi, how may I help you?\"\n);\n```\n\n## Current Symbolic Behavior\n\nThe engine normalizes a prompt, selects a deterministic symbolic rule, and returns the rule output with evidence link identifiers and indented Links Notation. It can also consume an explicit `ProbabilityStore`: append-only Bayesian-style evidence and Markov transition evidence rank symbolic candidate IDs before the temperature / clarify-vs-guess policy runs. This stays non-neural; evidence is Links Notation data with provenance, timestamps, cached-source fingerprints, and deterministic replay.\n\nSeed rules currently cover:\n\n- greetings and polite follow-ups: `Hi`, `Hello`, `Hey`, `I am fine, thank you`, `thanks`\n- hello world requests for Rust, Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, and C\n- open-ended software artifact requests such as extensions, plugins, bots, apps, and tools, first returning a Links Notation meaning record with a requirement graph, subtasks, delivery mode, approval gates, reasoning, and plan steps, then returning language-aware starter domain code after the user approves the plan\n- calculator-parsable math, unit, currency, percentage, and datetime prompts through `link-calculator`, with the local arithmetic evaluator retained for unsupported word-operator and binary-modulo syntax\n- URL requests such as `Navigate to github.com`, `fetch example.com`, and `Сделай запрос к google.com`; navigation prompts check CORS-readable frame-policy metadata and render an iframe only when `X-Frame-Options` and CSP `frame-ancestors` do not block embedding, while explicit fetch prompts attempt a browser `fetch()` first and use the same frame-policy check before any embedded fallback\n- web-search, information-search, and implicit research prompts such as `Search the web for Nikola Tesla`, `Найди яблоко в интернете`, `Найди информацию о Rust программировании`, `Rust programming के बारे में जानकारी खोजो`, `查找关于 Rust 编程的信息`, and `What is the most popular dataset for translation quality validation?`; the browser demo queries DuckDuckGo, Internet Archive, Wikipedia, Wikidata, and Wiktionary, then returns reciprocal-rank-fused links\n- merged definition prompts such as `Merge Wikipedia definitions of IIR`, which combine localized definition blocks for the same seed/Wikidata concept, deduplicate repeated facts, and cite every source language; use `--definition-fusion auto`, `FORMAL_AI_DEFINITION_FUSION=auto`, or the browser Settings control to make plain prompts like `What is IIR?` use the same fusion path\n- generic project lookups for GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket repository URLs plus default-on promotion for matching `link-assistant`, `link-foundation`, and `linksplatform` projects such as `What is Hive Mind?`, `Что такое Hive Mind?`, and `What is link-cli?`; promoted answers are generated from `data/seed/projects.lino` through the deterministic `formalize → summarize → deformalize` pipeline in `src/summarization/` (split into `mod.rs`, `markdown.rs`, and `dialog.rs`), which also drives README ingestion, multi-turn dialog summaries, and chat-title generation (see [ARCHITECTURE.md § 7.1](ARCHITECTURE.md#71-project-lookups-and-summarization))\n- behavior-rule inspection and dialog-local rule updates through `List behavior rules` (grouped by topic, each rendered as a `When X then Y` statement), `Show behavior rule unknown`, and the multilingual `When ... then ...` / `When ... do ...` / `When I say ... answer ...` grammar\n- unknown prompts, which return a larger learnable-rule fallback with exact commands for inspecting rules, teaching the current dialog, exporting memory, or reporting a missing built-in rule\n\nHello-world answers include execution metadata. Rust, Python, JavaScript, Go, and C examples are compiled or syntax-checked and run by the issue-8 local verification harness with captured output. TypeScript is returned with an explicit warning because `tsc` is not configured in the current repository runtime.\n\nNo GPU, neural network, remote model, or random sampling is used.\n\n## Dataset Seeds\n\nIssue #1 source indexes and seed prompts are stored as indented Links Notation in `data/`.\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\nrust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n```\n\nThe generator writes source, greeting, hello-world, and demo-dialog records. `.lino` files are kept below 1500 lines and validated by the unit tests.\n\n## Hive Mind Dataset Mining\n\nIssue #115 adds an operator script for mining GitHub evidence from\n`link-assistant/hive-mind` pull requests, issues, reviews, diffs, and Actions\nlogs into a case-study dataset. Keep this outside the seed tool registry; it is\na repository maintenance command, not an in-agent reasoning tool.\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --collect\n```\n\nThe script wraps `formal-ai github-logs plan|collect` with the focused Hive\nMind defaults used by `docs/case-studies/issue-115/`.\n\n## Development\n\n```bash\ncargo fmt --all -- --check\ncargo clippy --all-targets --all-features\ncargo test --all-features --verbose\ncargo test --doc --verbose\nrust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n```\n\nDecode an overlong prefilled GitHub issue URL into readable Markdown during\nreport-link triage:\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/decode-github-issue-url.rs --url 'https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/new?...'\n```\n\nSee [REQUIREMENTS.md](REQUIREMENTS.md) for the cumulative requirement matrix (now alongside [VISION.md](VISION.md)) and [docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md](docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md) for the collected research and implementation plan.",

Browser JavaScript dependencies are prebundled with Bun into `src/web/vendor.bundle.js`; run `bun install` once and `bun run build:web` after changing web dependencies. The companion connectivity diagnostics page lives in `src/web/tests/index.html` and is deployed at `/formal-ai/tests/`; it checks direct browser fetches, public knowledge APIs, iframe embeddability, and a configurable local `web-capture` proxy.\n\n### Desktop app\n\nThe desktop app lives in [`desktop/`](desktop/) and follows the same boundary as the browser and HTTP server. Electron starts a loopback `formal-ai serve` process, serves `src/web/` from a local static server, and loads the existing chat UI with a preload bridge that reports the API, graph, memory, and permission status.\n\n```bash\nnpm install --prefix desktop\ncargo build\nnpm run desktop:dev\nnpm run desktop:smoke\n```\n\nIn desktop mode, prompt sends use `POST /v1/chat/completions` on the local Rust API, and the network link points to `GET /v1/graph`. The same **Export memory** and **Import memory** controls read and write the full `formal_ai_bundle`; no separate desktop memory format exists. Agent mode remains off by default, and the desktop sidebar shows whether agent/tool-call actions are permission-gated or explicitly opted in.\n\nPackaging starts from the same shell:\n\n```bash\ncargo build --release\nnpm --prefix desktop run build\n```\n\nSet `FORMAL_AI_DESKTOP_BINARY=/path/to/formal-ai` before packaging to bundle a specific binary. Release builds copy the web assets and seed mirror into `desktop/dist-web/`, copy the binary into `desktop/bin/` when available, and produce OS artifacts under `desktop/release/`.\n\n### Full-memory export and import\n\nEvery interface produces the same self-contained Links Notation document by default. In the browser, the **Export memory** topbar button writes `formal-ai-memory.lino` as a complete `formal_ai_bundle` — the entire seed (rules, concepts, tools, multilingual responses), UI preferences, environment metadata, and the full append-only event log — so a single click is enough to reconstitute the session. **Import memory** auto-detects bundle vs legacy `demo_memory` files and surfaces migration suggestions when the imported seed version differs from the running app's. The CLI matches:\n\nNative Rust builds now select `doublets-rs` by default through the\n`doublets-native` feature. Links Notation stays the recovery and migration\nprojection: existing `demo_memory` logs and full `formal_ai_bundle` exports\nimport into the native store, export back to deterministic `.lino`, and can\nstill be handled by compiling with `--no-default-features` when a pure\n`MemoryStore` projection is needed.\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path full.lino           # default: full bundle\ncargo run -- memory export --from memory.lino --path events.lino --events-only  # legacy demo_memory\ncargo run -- memory import --path full.lino --into memory.lino           # accepts either format\ncargo run -- memory purge-deleted --path memory.lino --backup before-purge.lino --confirm\ncargo run -- memory reset --path memory.lino --backup before-reset.lino --confirm\ncargo run -- bundle export --path bundle.lino --memory memory.lino\ncargo run -- bundle import --path bundle.lino --into memory.lino\n```\n\nMemory normally remains append-only: deleting a conversation first records a\n`conversation_deleted` event and hides the thread. The explicit\n`purge-deleted` maintenance action physically removes every event for those\nsoft-deleted conversations, and `reset` clears the event log completely. Both\nbrowser actions show an export-first prompt and then an irreversible\nconfirmation prompt. The CLI refuses both destructive commands unless\n`--confirm` is present, and `--backup` writes a full `formal_ai_bundle` before\nthe memory file is changed.\n\nThe Rust library re-exports the same helpers — `export_memory_full`, `import_memory_full`, `suggest_memory_migrations`, `BundleInfo`, `ParsedBundle` — so embedders writing their own surface get the same defaults. The prefilled **Report issue** link records the dialog as a single compact `U:`/`A:` code block and points to [`docs/upload-memory.md`](docs/upload-memory.md) for attaching the full memory export (GitHub Gist or `.zip` workflow, plus redaction reminders) instead of repeating those instructions inline.\n\n### Teaching behavior in chat\n\nThe chat surface can explain and modify behavior rules without leaving the dialog. Behavior is surfaced as a series of `When X then Y` (or `When X do Y`) statements grouped by topic, and the same grammar can also update the dialog:\n\n```text\nList behavior rules\nShow behavior rule unknown\nList all facts you know about yourself\nWhen `Какая у тебя модель личности?` then `У меня символьная модель личности.`\nWhen I say `Какая у тебя модель личности?`, answer `У меня символьная модель личности.`\n```\n\n`List behavior rules` shows the current built-in routing rules, grouped by topic (Greetings, Farewells, Identity, Capabilities, Hello-world programs, Unknown fallback) and rendered as `When X then Y` statements. `Show behavior rule unknown` renders one rule as Links Notation with its topic, intent, match condition, response, source, and the canonical `when_then` statement. The `When X then Y` and `When X do Y` forms (and the explicit `When I say ... answer ...` form) record an append-only, dialog-local override, so the same prompt can answer differently in that conversation. The grammar is recognized in English (`When ... then ...`, `When ... do ...`, `If I ask ... reply ...`), Russian (`Когда ... тогда ...`, `Когда ... делай ...`, `Если ... то ...`), Hindi (`जब ... तब ...`, `जब ... तो ...`), and Chinese (`当 ... 时 ...`, `当 ... 则 ...`). Use **Export memory** to preserve that rule message with the session, or **Report issue** when the fact or rule should become part of the built-in seed.\n\n## Telegram Bot\n\nThe `formal-ai telegram` subcommand defaults to long polling and keeps the webhook server available as an opt-in mode. The CLI is configured through [`lino-arguments`](https://github.com/link-foundation/lino-arguments) (a clap-compatible derive), so every flag also reads from the matching environment variable and from `.lenv`/`.env` files in the working directory.\n\n### Long polling (default)\n\n```bash\nexport TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc\ncargo run -- telegram                                                   # polling by default\ncargo run -- telegram --mode polling \\\n  --timeout 30 --limit 100 \\\n  --allowed-updates message,edited_message\n```\n\nThe polling client shells out to `curl`, calls Telegram's `getUpdates`, advances the offset after each batch, and replies through `sendMessage` with HTML formatting. The same `FormalAiEngine` used by the library, HTTP API, and web demo is reused, so polling answers match the other surfaces.\n\nThe Docker image uses this polling command as its default `CMD`, so the\ncontainer starts the bot as soon as `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` is set. Commands that\nneed nested containers should run through the bundled `$` wrapper with\n`--isolated docker`; `start-command` records command output and metadata under\n`/tmp/start-command/logs/`. Issue #195 called this `--isolation docker`; the\ncurrent Link Foundation Start CLI documents the flag as `--isolated docker`.\n\n### Webhook (opt-in)\n\n```bash\ncargo run -- telegram --mode webhook --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n# or equivalently for backwards compatibility:\ncargo run -- serve --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8080\n\n# Docker override for webhook mode:\ndocker run --rm --privileged -p 8080:8080 \\\n  -e TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN=123:abc \\\n  formal-ai formal-ai telegram --mode webhook --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080\n```\n\nExpose the server through HTTPS and register the endpoint with Telegram:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s \"https://api.telegram.org/bot${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}/setWebhook\" \\\n  -d \"url=https://example.com/telegram/webhook\"\n```\n\nThe webhook accepts Telegram `message`, `edited_message`, `channel_post`, and `edited_channel_post` updates. It returns a direct Telegram `sendMessage` response for both private chats and group/channel chat IDs, using Telegram HTML formatting so code blocks survive the chat surface. This implementation does not store a bot token or perform outbound Telegram API calls from the webhook path; large file attachments require an outbound bot-client layer.\n\n## Rust Library\n\n```rust\nuse formal_ai::{create_chat_completion, ChatCompletionRequest, ChatMessage, MessageContent};\n\nlet request = ChatCompletionRequest {\n    model: None,\n    messages: vec![ChatMessage {\n        role: String::from(\"user\"),\n        content: MessageContent::Text(String::from(\"Hi\")),\n    }],\n    stream: false,\n    temperature: None,\n};\n\nlet completion = create_chat_completion(&request);\nassert_eq!(\n    completion.choices[0].message.content.plain_text(),\n    \"Hi, how may I help you?\"\n);\n```\n\n## Current Symbolic Behavior\n\nThe engine normalizes a prompt, selects a deterministic symbolic rule, and returns the rule output with evidence link identifiers and indented Links Notation. It can also consume an explicit `ProbabilityStore`: append-only Bayesian-style evidence and Markov transition evidence rank symbolic candidate IDs before the temperature / clarify-vs-guess policy runs. This stays non-neural; evidence is Links Notation data with provenance, timestamps, cached-source fingerprints, and deterministic replay.\n\nSeed rules currently cover:\n\n- greetings and polite follow-ups: `Hi`, `Hello`, `Hey`, `I am fine, thank you`, `thanks`\n- hello world requests for Rust, Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Go, and C\n- open-ended software artifact requests such as extensions, plugins, bots, apps, and tools, first returning a Links Notation meaning record with a requirement graph, subtasks, delivery mode, approval gates, reasoning, and plan steps, then returning language-aware starter domain code after the user approves the plan\n- calculator-parsable math, unit, currency, percentage, and datetime prompts through `link-calculator`, with the local arithmetic evaluator retained for unsupported word-operator and binary-modulo syntax\n- URL requests such as `Navigate to github.com`, `fetch example.com`, and `Сделай запрос к google.com`; navigation prompts check CORS-readable frame-policy metadata and render an iframe only when `X-Frame-Options` and CSP `frame-ancestors` do not block embedding, while explicit fetch prompts attempt a browser `fetch()` first and use the same frame-policy check before any embedded fallback\n- web-search, information-search, and implicit research prompts such as `Search the web for Nikola Tesla`, `Найди яблоко в интернете`, `Найди информацию о Rust программировании`, `Rust programming के बारे में जानकारी खोजो`, `查找关于 Rust 编程的信息`, and `What is the most popular dataset for translation quality validation?`; the browser demo queries DuckDuckGo, Internet Archive, Wikipedia, Wikidata, and Wiktionary, then returns reciprocal-rank-fused links\n- merged definition prompts such as `Merge Wikipedia definitions of IIR`, which combine localized definition blocks for the same seed/Wikidata concept, deduplicate repeated facts, and cite every source language; use `--definition-fusion auto`, `FORMAL_AI_DEFINITION_FUSION=auto`, or the browser Settings control to make plain prompts like `What is IIR?` use the same fusion path\n- generic project lookups for GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket repository URLs plus default-on promotion for matching `link-assistant`, `link-foundation`, and `linksplatform` projects such as `What is Hive Mind?`, `Что такое Hive Mind?`, and `What is link-cli?`; promoted answers are generated from `data/seed/projects.lino` through the deterministic `formalize → summarize → deformalize` pipeline in `src/summarization/` (split into `mod.rs`, `markdown.rs`, and `dialog.rs`), which also drives README ingestion, multi-turn dialog summaries, and chat-title generation (see [ARCHITECTURE.md § 7.1](ARCHITECTURE.md#71-project-lookups-and-summarization))\n- behavior-rule inspection and dialog-local rule updates through `List behavior rules` (grouped by topic, each rendered as a `When X then Y` statement), `Show behavior rule unknown`, and the multilingual `When ... then ...` / `When ... do ...` / `When I say ... answer ...` grammar\n- unknown prompts, which return a larger learnable-rule fallback with exact commands for inspecting rules, teaching the current dialog, exporting memory, or reporting a missing built-in rule\n\nHello-world answers include execution metadata. Rust, Python, JavaScript, Go, and C examples are compiled or syntax-checked and run by the issue-8 local verification harness with captured output. TypeScript is returned with an explicit warning because `tsc` is not configured in the current repository runtime.\n\nNo GPU, neural network, remote model, or random sampling is used.\n\n## Dataset Seeds\n\nIssue #1 source indexes and seed prompts are stored as indented Links Notation in `data/`.\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/download-datasets.rs\nrust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n```\n\nThe generator writes source, greeting, hello-world, and demo-dialog records. `.lino` files are kept below 1500 lines and validated by the unit tests.\n\n## Hive Mind Dataset Mining\n\nIssue #115 adds an operator script for mining GitHub evidence from\n`link-assistant/hive-mind` pull requests, issues, reviews, diffs, and Actions\nlogs into a case-study dataset. Keep this outside the seed tool registry; it is\na repository maintenance command, not an in-agent reasoning tool.\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --plan\nrust-script scripts/mine-hive-mind-dataset.rs --collect\n```\n\nThe script wraps `formal-ai github-logs plan|collect` with the focused Hive\nMind defaults used by `docs/case-studies/issue-115/`.\n\n## Development\n\n```bash\ncargo fmt --all -- --check\ncargo clippy --all-targets --all-features\ncargo test --all-features --verbose\ncargo test --doc --verbose\nrust-script scripts/check-file-size.rs\n```\n\nDecode an overlong prefilled GitHub issue URL into readable Markdown during\nreport-link triage:\n\n```bash\nrust-script scripts/decode-github-issue-url.rs --url 'https://github.com/link-assistant/formal-ai/issues/new?...'\n```\n\nSee [REQUIREMENTS.md](REQUIREMENTS.md) for the cumulative requirement matrix (now alongside [VISION.md](VISION.md)) and [docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md](docs/case-studies/issue-1/README.md) for the collected research and implementation plan.",

[2026-05-29T22:10:09.963Z] [INFO]           "content": "/**\n * sequence.js — the *sequence* combinator.\n *\n * Where `repeat` multiplies a single capability, `sequence` is the structure\n * that lets a blueprint compose genuinely *new* programs: an arbitrary, ordered\n * list of capability *steps* run one after another. Order is the first of the\n * three classic control-flow shapes — order, choice, repetition — that together\n * make the north star (\"any program is composable from the set of structures we\n * provide\") attainable.\n *\n * A step is a tagged capability spec — `{ kind: \"proof\", goal, term }`,\n * `{ kind: \"repeat\", count, body }`, a nested `sequence`/`branch`, … — whose\n * shape is validated by the shared composition model (`composition.js`). That\n * keeps the structures interchangeable and lets combinators nest arbitrarily.\n *\n * It follows the same small contract every structure shares:\n *\n *   - `createSequence(...)`   build a normalized spec\n *   - `validateSequence(...)` check the spec and return a structured result\n *   - `describeSequence(...)` produce a short human-readable description\n */\n\nimport { validateStep, describeStep } from \"./composition.js\";\n\nexport function createSequence({ steps, label } = {}) {\n  if (!Array.isArray(steps) || steps.length === 0) {\n    throw new Error(\"sequence: `steps` must be a non-empty array\");\n  }\n  const spec = { steps: Object.freeze(steps.slice()) };\n  if (label !== undefined) {\n    if (typeof label !== \"string\" || label.trim() === \"\") {\n      throw new Error(\"sequence: `label` must be a non-empty string when provided\");\n    }\n    spec.label = label.trim();\n  }\n  return Object.freeze(spec);\n}\n\nexport function validateSequence(spec) {\n  const errors = [];\n  let steps = [];\n  let label = null;\n  if (!spec || !Array.isArray(spec.steps) || spec.steps.length === 0) {\n    errors.push(\"sequence: `steps` must be a non-empty array\");\n  } else {\n    steps = spec.steps;\n    if (spec.label !== undefined) {\n      if (typeof spec.label !== \"string\" || spec.label.trim() === \"\") {\n        errors.push(\"sequence: `label` must be a non-empty string when provided\");\n      } else {\n        label = spec.label.trim();\n      }\n    }\n    steps.forEach((step, i) => {\n      const result = validateStep(step);\n      if (!result.valid) {\n        for (const err of result.errors) errors.push(`sequence[${i}]: ${err}`);\n      }\n    });\n  }\n  return Object.freeze({\n    steps: Object.freeze(steps),\n    label,\n    valid: errors.length === 0,\n    errors: Object.freeze(errors),\n    describe() {\n      if (errors.length) return \"sequence: <invalid>\";\n      const inner = steps.map((step) => describeStep(step)).join(\" → \");\n      return `sequence: ${inner}${label ? ` (${label})` : \"\"}`;\n    },\n  });\n}\n\nexport function describeSequence(spec) {\n  return validateSequence(spec).describe();\n}\n"

[2026-05-29T22:10:20.287Z] [INFO]           "content": "/**\n * branch.js — the *branch* (conditional) combinator.\n *\n * `branch` picks one of two capability *steps* based on a boolean predicate.\n * It is the *choice* control-flow shape: with `sequence` (order) and `repeat`\n * (repetition) it completes the small set of combinators from which, in\n * principle, any program can be assembled.\n *\n * Shape: `{ when: boolean, then: <step>, otherwise?: <step>, label? }`. When the\n * predicate is false and no `otherwise` step is given, the branch is a no-op.\n *\n * It follows the same small contract every structure shares:\n *\n *   - `createBranch(...)`   build a normalized spec\n *   - `validateBranch(...)` check the spec and return a structured result\n *   - `describeBranch(...)` produce a short human-readable description\n */\n\nimport { validateStep, describeStep } from \"./composition.js\";\n\nexport function createBranch({ when, then, otherwise, label } = {}) {\n  if (typeof when !== \"boolean\") {\n    throw new Error(\"branch: `when` must be a boolean\");\n  }\n  if (then === undefined) {\n    throw new Error(\"branch: `then` step is required\");\n  }\n  const spec = { when, then };\n  if (otherwise !== undefined) spec.otherwise = otherwise;\n  if (label !== undefined) {\n    if (typeof label !== \"string\" || label.trim() === \"\") {\n      throw new Error(\"branch: `label` must be a non-empty string when provided\");\n    }\n    spec.label = label.trim();\n  }\n  return Object.freeze(spec);\n}\n\nexport function validateBranch(spec) {\n  const errors = [];\n  let label = null;\n  if (!spec || typeof spec.when !== \"boolean\") {\n    errors.push(\"branch: `when` must be a boolean\");\n  }\n  if (!spec || spec.then === undefined) {\n    errors.push(\"branch: `then` step is required\");\n  }\n  if (spec && spec.label !== undefined) {\n    if (typeof spec.label !== \"string\" || spec.label.trim() === \"\") {\n      errors.push(\"branch: `label` must be a non-empty string when provided\");\n    } else {\n      label = spec.label.trim();\n    }\n  }\n  if (spec && spec.then !== undefined) {\n    const result = validateStep(spec.then);\n    if (!result.valid) {\n      for (const err of result.errors) errors.push(`branch.then: ${err}`);\n    }\n  }\n  if (spec && spec.otherwise !== undefined) {\n    const result = validateStep(spec.otherwise);\n    if (!result.valid) {\n      for (const err of result.errors) errors.push(`branch.otherwise: ${err}`);\n    }\n  }\n  return Object.freeze({\n    when: spec && typeof spec.when === \"boolean\" ? spec.when : null,\n    label,\n    valid: errors.length === 0,\n    errors: Object.freeze(errors),\n    describe() {\n      if (errors.length) return \"branch: <invalid>\";\n      const thenText = describeStep(spec.then);\n      const elseText = spec.otherwise !== undefined ? describeStep(spec.otherwise) : \"∅\";\n      return `branch: ${spec.when ? \"then\" : \"else\"} [${thenText} | ${elseText}]${label ? ` (${label})` : \"\"}`;\n    },\n  });\n}\n\nexport function describeBranch(spec) {\n  return validateBranch(spec).describe();\n}\n"

[2026-05-29T22:10:33.477Z] [INFO]           "content": "/**\n * composition.js — the shared *step* model that ties the structures together.\n *\n * A blueprint composes capabilities; the combinators (`sequence`, `branch`)\n * compose *steps*. A step is just a tagged capability spec:\n *\n *   { kind: \"proof\",    goal, term }\n *   { kind: \"repeat\",   count, label?, body: <step> }\n *   { kind: \"sequence\", steps: [<step>, ...] }\n *   { kind: \"branch\",   when, then: <step>, otherwise?: <step> }\n *\n * This module is the small, shared contract the combinators rely on: a single\n * place that knows how to validate, describe, and run *any* step by dispatching\n * on its `kind`. Because combinators only ever go through `validateStep` /\n * `describeStep` / `runStep`, they can nest arbitrarily without knowing each\n * other's internals — which is what keeps the set of structures open and\n * uniformly composable.\n *\n * Note the deliberate circular import with the combinator modules: `sequence`\n * and `branch` import `validateStep`/`describeStep`/`runStep` from here, and we\n * import their validate/describe/run from there. ES modules resolve this safely\n * because every reference is used at call time, never at module-eval time.\n */\n\nimport { validateProof, describeProof } from \"./proof.js\";\nimport { validateRepeat, describeRepeat } from \"./repeat.js\";\nimport { validateSequence, describeSequence } from \"./sequence.js\";\nimport { validateBranch, describeBranch } from \"./branch.js\";\nimport { runProof } from \"./proof_runtime.js\";\nimport { runRepeat } from \"./repeat_runtime.js\";\nimport { runSequence } from \"./sequence_runtime.js\";\nimport { runBranch } from \"./branch_runtime.js\";\n\nfunction invalidStep(message) {\n  return Object.freeze({ valid: false, errors: Object.freeze([message]) });\n}\n\nexport function validateStep(step) {\n  if (!step || typeof step !== \"object\" || typeof step.kind !== \"string\") {\n    return invalidStep(\"step: `kind` must be a string\");\n  }\n  switch (step.kind) {\n    case \"proof\":\n      return validateProof(step);\n    case \"repeat\": {\n      const errors = [];\n      const repeat = validateRepeat(step);\n      if (!repeat.valid) errors.push(...repeat.errors);\n      if (step.body === undefined) {\n        errors.push(\"repeat: `body` step is required\");\n      } else {\n        const body = validateStep(step.body);\n        if (!body.valid) {\n          for (const err of body.errors) errors.push(`repeat.body: ${err}`);\n        }\n      }\n      return Object.freeze({\n        valid: errors.length === 0,\n        errors: Object.freeze(errors),\n      });\n    }\n    case \"sequence\":\n      return validateSequence(step);\n    case \"branch\":\n      return validateBranch(step);\n    default:\n      return invalidStep(`step: unknown kind \"${step.kind}\"`);\n  }\n}\n\nexport function describeStep(step) {\n  if (!step || typeof step !== \"object\" || typeof step.kind !== \"string\") {\n    return \"step: <invalid>\";\n  }\n  switch (step.kind) {\n    case \"proof\":\n      return describeProof(step);\n    case \"repeat\":\n      return `${describeRepeat(step)}[${step.body ? describeStep(step.body) : \"∅\"}]`;\n    case \"sequence\":\n      return describeSequence(step);\n    case \"branch\":\n      return describeBranch(step);\n    default:\n      return `step: unknown kind \"${step.kind}\"`;\n  }\n}\n\nexport function runStep(step) {\n  const validated = validateStep(step);\n  if (!validated.valid) {\n    return Object.freeze({\n      kind: step && typeof step.kind === \"string\" ? step.kind : \"step\",\n      ok: false,\n      errors: validated.errors,\n    });\n  }\n  switch (step.kind) {\n    case \"proof\":\n      return runProof(step);\n    case \"repeat\":\n      return runRepeat(step, runStep(step.body));\n    case \"sequence\":\n      return runSequence(step);\n    case \"branch\":\n      return runBranch(step);\n    default:\n      return Object.freeze({\n        kind: \"step\",\n        ok: false,\n        errors: Object.freeze([`step: unknown kind \"${step.kind}\"`]),\n      });\n  }\n}\n"

The sequence\n * is `ok` only when every step is `ok`, so a failure anywhere is observable in\n * the program report.\n */\n\nimport { validateSequence } from \"./sequence.js\";\nimport { runStep } from \"./composition.js\";\n\nexport function runSequence(spec) {\n  const result = validateSequence(spec);\n  if (!result.valid) {\n    return Object.freeze({\n      kind: \"sequence\",\n      ok: false,\n      errors: result.errors,\n    });\n  }\n  const steps = result.steps.map((step) => runStep(step));\n  const ok = steps.every((step) => step.ok);\n  return Object.freeze({\n    kind: \"sequence\",\n    ok,\n    label: result.label ?? null,\n    steps: Object.freeze(steps),\n    detail: `ran ${steps.length} step(s) in order`,\n  });\n}\n"

[2026-05-29T22:10:42.464Z] [INFO]           "content": "/**\n * branch_runtime.js — executes the branch combinator.\n *\n * Running a branch evaluates the boolean predicate and runs the chosen step\n * through the shared step dispatcher. When the predicate is false and no\n * `otherwise` step was provided, the branch is a no-op that still reports `ok`,\n * recording which side was taken.\n */\n\nimport { validateBranch } from \"./branch.js\";\nimport { runStep } from \"./composition.js\";\n\nexport function runBranch(spec) {\n  const result = validateBranch(spec);\n  if (!result.valid) {\n    return Object.freeze({\n      kind: \"branch\",\n      ok: false,\n      errors: result.errors,\n    });\n  }\n  const taken = result.when ? \"then\" : \"otherwise\";\n  const chosen = result.when ? spec.then : spec.otherwise;\n  const inner = chosen !== undefined ? runStep(chosen) : null;\n  return Object.freeze({\n    kind: \"branch\",\n    ok: inner ? inner.ok : true,\n    taken,\n    result: inner,\n    detail: inner ? `took ${taken} branch` : \"took empty else branch\",\n  });\n}\n"

[2026-05-29T22:11:27.228Z] [INFO]           "content": "/**\n * case_study.js — the showcase programs.\n *\n * This module assembles showcase programs from the compositional structures and\n * runs them end-to-end. Two programs are shown:\n *\n *   1. `RepeatProof`   — the original program: a `proof` multiplied by `repeat`,\n *                        with human-readable `comments` riding along.\n *   2. `ProofPipeline` — a program that could *not* be expressed before this\n *                        set grew: an ordered `sequence` of steps that mixes a\n *                        `branch` (choice) and a `repeat` (repetition). It is the\n *                        concrete payoff of the north star — \"any program is\n *                        composable from the set of structures we provide.\"\n *\n * Run it with:  node src/case_study.js\n */\n\nimport { createProof } from \"./proof.js\";\nimport { createRepeat } from \"./repeat.js\";\nimport { createComment } from \"./comments.js\";\nimport { createSequence } from \"./sequence.js\";\nimport { composeBlueprint } from \"./blueprint.js\";\nimport { runBlueprint } from \"./blueprint_dispatch.js\";\n\nfunction buildRepeatProof() {\n  return {\n    proof: createProof({ goal: \"G\", term: \"t\" }),\n    repeat: createRepeat({ count: 3 }),\n    comments: [\n      createComment({ id: \"c1\", text: \"prove G\", target: \"proof\" }),\n      createComment({ id: \"c2\", text: \"repeat 3x\", target: \"repeat\" }),\n    ],\n  };\n}\n\nexport function runCaseStudy() {\n  const spec = buildRepeatProof();\n  const blueprint = composeBlueprint(spec);\n  const report = runBlueprint(blueprint);\n  return { spec, blueprint, report };\n}\n\n/**\n * `ProofPipeline` composes the *new* combinators: an ordered sequence whose\n * steps are a proof, a conditional branch, and a repeated proof. None of this\n * was expressible when the vocabulary was just `proof` + `repeat`.\n */\nfunction buildProofPipeline() {\n  return {\n    sequence: createSequence({\n      label: \"ProofPipeline\",\n      steps: [\n        { kind: \"proof\", goal: \"A ⇒ A\", term: \"λx.x\" },\n        {\n          kind: \"branch\",\n          when: true,\n          then: { kind: \"proof\", goal: \"B\", term: \"b\" },\n          otherwise: { kind: \"proof\", goal: \"C\", term: \"c\" },\n        },\n        {\n          kind: \"repeat\",\n          count: 2,\n          label: \"twice\",\n          body: { kind: \"proof\", goal: \"D\", term: \"d\" },\n        },\n      ],\n    }),\n    comments: [\n      createComment({ id: \"p1\", text: \"ordered proof pipeline\", target: \"sequence\" }),\n    ],\n  };\n}\n\nexport function runPipelineCaseStudy() {\n  const spec = buildProofPipeline();\n  const blueprint = composeBlueprint(spec);\n  const report = runBlueprint(blueprint);\n  return { spec, blueprint, report };\n}\n\nexport function formatReport(report, title = \"RepeatProof\") {\n  const lines = [];\n  lines.push(`${title} — composition report`);\n  lines.push(\"=\".repeat(`${title} — composition report`.length));\n  for (const program of report.programs) {\n    const detail = program.detail ?? (program.ok ? \"ok\" : \"failed\");\n    lines.push(`• ${program.kind}: ${detail}`);\n  }\n  return lines.join(\"\\n\");\n}\n\nif (import.meta.url === `file://${process.argv[1]}`) {\n  const repeatProof = runCaseStudy();\n  const pipeline = runPipelineCaseStudy();\n  // eslint-disable-next-line no-console\n  console.log(formatReport(repeatProof.report, \"RepeatProof\"));\n  // eslint-disable-next-line no-console\n  console.log(\"\");\n  // eslint-disable-next-line no-console\n  console.log(formatReport(pipeline.report, \"ProofPipeline\"));\n}\n"

They\ncompose *steps* (tagged capability specs) through a small shared model in\n`src/composition.js`, so they nest arbitrarily. This is what moves the project\ntoward its north star: *any program is composable from the set of structures we\nprovide.*"

[2026-05-29T22:14:46.560Z] [INFO]           "old_string": "## [Unreleased]\n\n### Added\n\n- **comments** compositional structure (`src/comments.js`, `src/comments_runtime.js`):\n  human-readable annotations that travel with a composition. Documentation\n  becomes a first-class structure — annotations are validated and threaded into\n  the program report alongside every other capability.\n- Blueprint now wires a `comments` slot next to `proof` and `repeat`, so a\n  composition can carry validated annotations end-to-end.\n- Case study (`RepeatProof`) attaches two comments and the formatted report is\n  unchanged in spirit while documentation now rides along with the program.\n- Tests for the comments structure, its runtime, blueprint integration, and an\n  end-to-end composition path that mirrors the existing `proof`/`repeat` style.\n\n### Changed\n\n- README documents the comments structure in the compositional vocabulary table\n  and project layout.",
[2026-05-29T22:14:46.560Z] [INFO]           "new_string": "## [Unreleased]\n\n### Added\n\n- **sequence** combinator (`src/sequence.js`, `src/sequence_runtime.js`): runs an\n  ordered list of capability *steps* one after another (the *order* control-flow\n  shape). This is the structure that lets a blueprint compose genuinely new\n  programs instead of the single fixed `proof`→`repeat` wiring.\n- **branch** combinator (`src/branch.js`, `src/branch_runtime.js`): chooses one\n  of two capability steps from a boolean predicate (the *choice* control-flow\n  shape). Together with `repeat` (repetition) and `sequence` (order) the\n  vocabulary now covers the classic control-flow shapes.\n- **composition** model (`src/composition.js`): the shared *step* contract\n  (`validateStep` / `describeStep` / `runStep`) the combinators dispatch through,\n  so `proof`, `repeat`, `sequence`, and `branch` nest arbitrarily.\n- Blueprint now wires `sequence` and `branch` slots next to `proof`, `repeat`,\n  and `comments`, and the dispatcher runs them end-to-end.\n- New showcase program `ProofPipeline` (`src/case_study.js`): an ordered sequence\n  that mixes a branch and a repeated proof — a program that could not be\n  expressed when the vocabulary was only `proof` + `repeat`.\n- **comments** compositional structure (`src/comments.js`, `src/comments_runtime.js`):\n  human-readable annotations that travel with a composition. Documentation\n  becomes a first-class structure — annotations are validated and threaded into\n  the program report alongside every other capability.\n- Tests for the new combinators, the shared step model, blueprint integration,\n  the comments structure, and end-to-end composition paths that mirror the\n  existing `proof`/`repeat` style.\n\n### Fixed\n\n- `node src/case_study.js` crashed with `ReferenceError: report is not defined`\n  and rendered `• undefined` rows; the showcase entry point now runs and prints a\n  concrete report for both `RepeatProof` and `ProofPipeline`.\n\n### Changed\n\n- README documents the new combinators (order/choice/repetition) and the shared\n  composition model in the compositional vocabulary table, showcase section, and\n  project layout."

[2026-05-29T22:16:06.526Z] [INFO]           "file_path": "/tmp/pr_body.md",
[2026-05-29T22:16:06.526Z] [INFO]           "content": "# Compose programs on a richer set of structures (#340)\n\n## What this PR does\n\nExpands the **compositional vocabulary** so that the blueprint can assemble\ngenuinely *new programs*, not just the single fixed `proof`→`repeat` wiring.\n\nFollowing review feedback (\"look more carefully at what is asked in the issue,\nwhat programs we already could compose, and what set of structures\"), the focus\nis on structures that actually broaden the set of composable **programs** — the\nissue's north star: *any program is composable from the set of structures we\nprovide.*\n\n## Why the first pass wasn't enough\n\nThe initial version added **comments**. Comments are a legitimate first-class\nstructure (documentation-as-data), but — as its own runtime says — *\"running\ncomments doesn't change control flow.\"* They annotate a program; they do not\nlet you compose a program you couldn't compose before. Before this PR the only\nprogram expressible was `RepeatProof` (proof + repeat).\n\n## The structures added\n\nThe classic control-flow shapes are now all present as combinators:\n\n| Shape | Structure | Module |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| repetition | `repeat` (pre-existing) | `src/repeat.js` |\n| **order** | **`sequence`** | `src/sequence.js`, `src/sequence_runtime.js` |\n| **choice** | **`branch`** | `src/branch.js`, `src/branch_runtime.js` |\n\n- **`sequence`** runs an ordered list of capability *steps* one after another,\n  threading results into a single report.\n- **`branch`** chooses one of two steps from a boolean predicate.\n- **`composition.js`** is the shared *step* model (`validateStep` /\n  `describeStep` / `runStep`) the combinators dispatch through, so `proof`,\n  `repeat`, `sequence`, and `branch` **nest arbitrarily**. A step is a tagged\n  capability spec, e.g. `{ kind: \"proof\", goal, term }` or\n  `{ kind: \"repeat\", count, body: <step> }`.\n\nAll four are wired into `blueprint.js` (new `sequence`/`branch` slots) and run by\n`blueprint_dispatch.js`, the same way every other capability is.\n\n## A program that couldn't be composed before\n\nNew showcase `ProofPipeline` in `src/case_study.js` — an ordered `sequence` that\nmixes a `branch` (choice) and a `repeat` (repetition):\n\n```\nProofPipeline — composition report\n=================================\n• sequence: ran 3 step(s) in order\n• comments: threaded 1 annotation(s)\n```\n\n## Bug fixed along the way\n\n`node src/case_study.js` was crashing with\n`ReferenceError: report is not defined` (and rendering `• undefined` rows). The\nshowcase entry point now runs and prints concrete reports for both\n`RepeatProof` and `ProofPipeline`.\n\n## How to verify\n\n```bash\nnpm test                 # 148 tests pass\nnode src/case_study.js   # prints RepeatProof + ProofPipeline reports\nnpm run lint             # prettier + eslint clean\n```\n\n## Tests\n\n- `src/sequence.test.js`, `src/branch.test.js` — structure contract tests\n  mirroring `proof`/`repeat`.\n- `src/composition.test.js` — the shared step model (validate/describe/run,\n  nesting, invalid kinds).\n- `src/pipeline_e2e.test.js` — sequence/branch compose end-to-end through the\n  public blueprint engine, including a nested pipeline and a failure path.\n- `src/case_study.test.js` — the new `ProofPipeline` showcase and the fixed\n  `formatReport`.\n\n## Docs\n\n- README: combinators added to the vocabulary table (order/choice/repetition),\n  both showcase programs documented, project layout updated.\n- CHANGELOG: new `### Added` / `### Fixed` / `### Changed` entries under\n  `[Unreleased]`.\n"
